recipeTitle: Spaghetti Aglio Olio With Zucchini Recipe
commentBody: Completely agree w using fresh garlic, which makes this dish seasonal. Garlic is being harvested now in late june and over the next few weeks in the mid-altantic states depending on the varietal and available at farmers market (if you don't grow it yourself). author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Spaghetti Aglio Olio With Zucchini Recipe
commentBody: Suggest tossing unsalted thick zucchini slices or batons in olive oil, searing both sides quickly on a flat cast iron griddle while the pasta cooks and then folding into the flavored oil. It's better to pull the pasta out of the water with tongs, if using spaghetti, or a spider for short pasta and add immediately to flavored oil so it doesn't stand in a colander and stiffen. Locatelli will stand up better to these flavors.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Spaghetti Aglio Olio With Zucchini Recipe
commentBody:
This is one of those recipes that I read and think “how good can that be?” But I had all of the ingredients, so it became dinner.  The dish was stunning - really.  
The recipe is simple, but for this cook, not easy, but well worth the effort to make something stand out.  We altered the recipe only for garlic tolerance and heat tolerance.
Try it with all of that summer zucchini and the spaghetti in your pantry and the good olive oil.  I think you’ll be glad you did.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Spaghetti Aglio Olio With Zucchini Recipe
commentBody: If you want to get maximum flavor out of every ingredient in this dish, steep the garlic (and some chili flakes or thinly sliced red peppers, if using.) This technique lets the garlic and chili aromatics fully permeate the oil, adding a delicious dimension that is lacking in a hastily prepared dish. As a second step, I would salt and let those wet zucchini batons weep out for the same period of time, then squeeze them as dry as possible before frying on their own. The proceed with the recipe.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Hi Eric, can I make a general plea from readers who don’t live in the USA? (I’m in Australia). We don’t have anything called ‘kosher’ salt. And by giving quantities by volume we have no idea how much salt to use. So please - to all American recipe writers - give us quantities - especially of salt - in grams. 
Looking forward to trying the recipe. Seems like a refinement of my longtime fave, from Judy Rogers’ Zuni Café Cookbook.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I have to say this blew me away.  Well done Eric.  I actually adapted it for two bone-in breasts which I elevated on top of the aromatics and cooked for about 35 minutes.  I still ended up using the same amount water which made for an excellent, deeply caramelized jus.  Crispy skin, unbelievably moist and flavorful.  Paired with roasted sweet potato which worked very well.  The pan roasted onion also came out sweet and delicious and became part of the meal.  I’ll be doing this again for sure.  Bravo.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: After my husband's heart surgery I started roasting a whole chicken each week to avoid buying the overly-processed and salty lunch meats he used to use for sandwiches. This recipe caught my eye because the roasting ingredients were so different. It is by far the most tasty roasted chicken I've ever prepared. I used less salt than the recipe called for, but followed the rest of the recipe as written. Because my schedule allowed, I dry brined the chicken in the refrigerator overnight. Try it!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: This was excellent. My chicken was on the larger size, and although it got up to temp in an hour and 15 minutes, because of the brining I should have left it in for another 15 or so, will do next time. Used five spice powder (1 tsp) instead of fennel, cinnamon, and cloves as I was feeling lazy and added ground coriander per recipe. Served with sriracha, nuoc cham, chunks of green cabbage, romaine lettuce leaves, sliced cucumber. Next time will add daikon and carrot quick pickles. This is dinner-party worthy, would serve at a party with the above and a rice-noodle salad with fresh herbs, peanuts, jalapeno, and fried shallots on top. Perfect summer party fare.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Here's a question on behalf of the very large numbers of readers who are under physicians' orders to limit sodium. Brined meat is one of the things that is strictly off limits. So . . . would it work to rub the chicken just with the spices mentioned here and not with the salt? Would I need to adjust cooking times if I don't brine the bird first? 

It is very discouraging to me that so very many recipes now call for brining and offer no suggestions for what to do if you have to limit sodium.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Talk about the finer points of salt misses the point. If you haven't started with the proper chicken, meaning air chilled, you are doomed, regardless of recipe. Roast chicken is absurdly simple. Rub the chicken--air chilled--with olive oil, sprinkle with salt (any kind will do) and pepper and thyme (dried is fine), put a quarter of fresh lemon and an equal amount of onion in the cavity and roast at 375 degrees at 20 minutes per pound for a bird weighing more than 4 pounds. Easy peasey.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I’ve made this twice now, and I only wish I could give it more than 5 stars. A couple tips: go for the full 24 hour brine so the flavor can really permeate the meat. My organic Trader Joe’s chicken weighed 6 pounds so I upped the spice blend ingredients by half and am glad I did. Use the 9x13 sheet pan for sure, and don’t think twice about the 1/4 cup of water. The skin was gorgeous and crispy. I suspect trussing the bird might have helped the breast stay so moist - usually cooking the thighs to 175 feels like you’re sacrificing the breast. Not so here. Basted every 15 minutes. Some commenters said it was too dry to baste - maybe you used a larger pan?

One guest said it was the best chicken she’s ever eaten. Served with roasted bok choy with oyster sauce and garlic, ginger and scallions. This is perfect for a special occasion. Home run, Eric!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Due to an obligation that I had forgotten about, I had to make this a day prior to my original plan, which meant my chicken only got a 1.5 hour brine instead of the intended overnight. Dear reader! Nevertheless! This is EASILY in the top 5 (if not 3) dinners I have ever produced. Can’t wait to do it again with the overnight brine. My only adjustment was to add a few pieces of star anise. Cooking time was on the money. Spooned the pan juices over rice noodles and served with nouc cham, tons of herbs, lime, toasted cashews, and sriracha. Sautéed bok choy with oyster sauce on the side. Oh and the house smelled incredible. I ate this 3 hours ago and writing this review is making me hungry for it all over again.

commentBody: Eric Kim does it again. I made this using a 4lb chicken, brined for 2 hours, roasted for 75 mins, and let rest for 30 mins. Just so tasty and the house smelled amazing. Love the spice blend!! Our chicken didn’t have too much drippings but we added some more liquid to the roasting pan to make a sauce. It tasted great on the rice. Just make it.

commentBody:

The second time, I made a Peking duck inspired brine and served the chicken with pancakes, slivered scallions and cucumbers, mint, basil and cilantro leaves, and some hoisin sauce. Everyone loved it. This is the brine I used:
1.5 teaspoons fine-grain salt
¾ teaspoon five spice powder
¼ teaspoon garlic powder
¼ teaspoon ground white pepper
1 teaspoon soy sauce
1 teaspoon rice wine (could use dry sherry)

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Some time ago a cooking column discussed the myth of long 'brines' with either immersing the bird of choice in a water solution, or 'dry brining' with just sprinkling and rubbing it on.

The article, which I wasn't able to reference except by take-away memory, discussed length of time and how deeply, if at all, the salt penetrated into the flesh.

I wonder if a good study by food scientists has investigated this, and it is worth trying to find enough room in the fridge to let a bird overnight?

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I made this with a griller pack that I had in the freezer. the pack included four drumsticks and two chicken breast halves. I cut the breast halves in half so they were approximately the same size as the drumsticks. Because there was so much more surface area, I doubled the seasonings except for the salt. I also added an extra onion because I love cooked onions. The chicken came out amazing like crispy and delicious. It only took an hour to cook because of the smaller pieces. I will definitely make this again. My kitchen smelled amazing!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This was delicious. However, the cooking procedure did not scale down well for me. I had one thigh-leg piece and cooked it in a small pan in the oven. While the onion was delicious to eat, the broth just created a burnt mess in my pan. I garnished with cilantro, as well as basil and mint from my garden. I don’t think I would use the lime again, and I might throw some carrots in when roasting next time.

I plan on making this again with a whole chicken. Based on my experience tonight with one piece of chicken, I will probably double or triple the dry seasoning. I’m also going to butterfly the chicken, which will affect how long it takes to cook, of course. If I do any of this, I’ll report back!

commentBody: The estimated time to make this - 1.5 hours - is way off.  Cooking it alone can take 1.5 hours.  And then there's a 30 minute rest, which brings you to 2 hours. So a minimum of 2 hours more or 4.5 hours altogether.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I have a small farmer 5 minutes away who raises his chickens so expertly that they star on the cutting-edge Honolulu restaurant menus. I've been working on my roast chicken with mediocre results. Until this recipe by Eric Kim.  The depth and boldness of flavor, the perfection of the texture due to the dry brine, and the lusciousness of the bite blew me away.  I used calamansi instead of lime. My sides: yellow dal basmati rice w garam masala; cauliflower w Jalapenos, mustard seed, cumin,coconut.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Parchment-Steamed Fish With Buttered Radishes Recipe
commentBody:
Made this last night for 3 of us, as a trial run for a dinner party as it looks elegant and love that you can prep before and then pop in the oven and plate when needed.
I needs more oomph. I added a bit of lemongrass paste and would up it to about 1/2 tsp. Per portion.  I used 8 oz halibut filets which were a nice although pricey choice. Also could use more pepper. 
I ended up squeezing fresh lime on the filet, once opened after cooking. 
It’s a very pretty dish, sure to impress, but will keep experimenting with more spice to add more flavor.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Broiled Wild Salmon and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: I used regular old farmed Atlantic salmon, cut into roughly square pieces (it was a tail end so a bit thinner). They cooked perfectly in about 10 minutes with no flipping, but the veg needed more time after removing the fish from the sheet pan. No biggie, but when I make this again I will give the veg a head start, or possibly use 2 trays and cook the veg first separately. And I WILL make it again, maybe adding sliced fennel to the veg mix. My husband and I were both shocked at how delicious the salmon was with just S&P and mayo. This is a posterchild recipe for “simple can be really really good.”

recipeTitle: Broiled Wild Salmon and Peppers Recipe
commentBody: Been preparing different types of salmon by alternate methods over many years and never used mayonnaise as an ingredient. Indeed, I use it sparingly but curiosity got the best of me. I experimented on typically available salmon. But I was beyond surprised; absolutely delicious and received glowing comments.  Vegetables were wonderful and one can get even more creative here. Yes, capers add a great dimension. Eric Kim accurately described the fast cooking for a crisp bubbly skin over perfectly cooked salmon. Can’t wait to try with Alaskan wild caught salmon which is readily available in my area.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Bomdong Bibimbap (Spring Cabbage, Rice and Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: A wonderful dish! I made this last night with Dew Drop cabbage, an artisanal petite cabbage with a sweet flavor which has been appearing sporadically at our local Trader Joe's (and we aren't close to a source for bomdong). I put the cabbage in a steaming basket and put it in the Instant Pot over the just-cooked rice to let the cabbage warm up a little while I mixed the sauce and fried the eggs. I did 50/50 gochugaru and gochugang but next time will do all gochugaru.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Bomdong Bibimbap (Spring Cabbage, Rice and Eggs) Recipe
commentBody: This was so quick and so good I felt compelled to leave a comment.  Don’t miss out!  I made adjustments based on what I had available and it still came out great, so much so I can’t wait to have it again.  I subbed soy sauce for fish sauce, left out the sesame seeds, used up some leftover salad  (cucumbers, white beans, roasted red peppers, and broccoli, all bite sized) instead of cutting up cabbage, and had it with leftover brown rice and crispy fried eggs.  I eyeballed the sauce ingredients. And even with these shortcuts it was still great!!!!.

commentBody: I will try this with steamed jasmine rice, frozen (available at Trader Joe’s and also at Woodman’s) and cooked in the microwave. Yes, it costs more— but taken with how much poorly cooked rice I have had to throw away, for me it balances out. Add in the savings in time and it’s a huge win, especially for the vegetarian in our household! No, I was not paid for this endorsement :-)

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Braised Kimchi Chicken With Sweet Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Excellent spin on Kimchi stew. Loved it but my wife found butter an odd flavor in a Korean dish. Butter chicken meets kimchi? Blanching the chicken seemed odd for a spicy braise but I did it. Once assembled and cooking, there was more than enough liquid; cooking it down resulted in well done chicken legs. So my bad; limit added water. To the "will it be too hot" folks: if you don't like spicy food, don't cook recipes with spicy ingredients. Gochugaru is not very hot, but kimchi can be. Taste it!

recipeTitle: Braised Kimchi Chicken With Sweet Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: IMO this recipe could be massively simplified with buying a jar of kimchi you like. Jars of kimchi typically come with a lot of kimchi juice and already flavored water ‘stock’ that you can simply pour in the pot to braise instead of going through the trouble of buying all these ingredients and making the stock itself. The magic of kimchi flavor is lost since you’re not using the fermented version.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Braised Kimchi Chicken With Sweet Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I loved the deep and complex flavors in the broth. I definitely recommend this over rice.   I never understood the differences with sweet potato variations, but the Asian ones definitely hold up better in the longer braise. However, I definitely had to cook it slightly longer than the recommended 30 minutes for the potatoes to become tender enough. Might try adding more kimchi or napa cabbage for more vegetables next time.  This is a keeper in our rotation now.

commentBody:
My 7 and 10 year old sons loved making these- they had a lot of fun rolling the pistachio balls in the marshmallow coating. We all enjoyed eating it. The straight cocoa powder on the outer layer was a little bitter for the kids, so next time I might mix the cocoa powder with some powdered sugar before rolling. 

We also thought these were a fun Easter treat- the brown exterior looks like the rock, representing the stone rolled over the tomb, with a green inside to represent spring/ renewal.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I know this will get groans because I didn’t make the recipe. But the original Dubai chocolate adds tahini to the pistachio so it’s not so sweet and more complex. Many of the copies of this trend exported it without this and i think you end up missing an important note of Dubai chocolate flavour. Of course, here we get a Korean adaptation and I’m all for it but for those who are interested, just my 2 cents!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: My "eight perfect rounds" looked more like misshapen, pockmarked golfballs, so my technique needs work. The marshmallow shell cools quickly and is devilishly difficult to manipulate, even with super-greased hands. (Don't be tempted to double the recipe; time is of the essence.) These were tasty, but not sure they rewarded the effort. The upside is, I now have 3/4 of a box of kataifi in the freezer for my next Dubai chocolate adventure, though it probably won't be Dujjonku.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
My favorite part of this experience was when I called while foods to see if they had kataifi and the lady on line signed deeply and said “ for the pistachio cream cookies?” 
That being said, I thought the recipe came together easily, although my result was less green than the photos. I didn’t wait the requisite ten minutes for the marshmallow coating to cool, and glad I didn’t because it was harder and harder to work with. They weren’t “perfect rounds” but still delicious. 
A word of caution: according to my husband after the first bite I exhaled a cloud of cocoa powder- may have been a little heavy handed….

commentBody: Tasty and surprisingly sweet for how little sugar/chocolate you're using across the entire recipe. And no baking! The marshmallow coating was the hardest part. I used dried kataifi, not frozen and it worked fine, though maybe that's why my pistachio paste was drier than expected.

commentBody: These were delicious and a lot of fun. I added about a tbsp of tahini, and it made a huge difference. I also used mini marshmallows and they were fine. author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Bean and Vegetable Japanese Curry Recipe
commentBody: This was my first time using curry bricks, and I'm a convert! This is a great "clean-out-the-fridge" recipe, so don't feel bound by the listed ingredients. I doubled the recipe in order to freeze the leftovers, and used one medium and one hot S&B brick. I was short on carrots, so added a peeled and diced sweet potato. I had half a head of cabbage left over, so I chopped and sauteed that along with the onion before adding the water. This was an easy and substantial weeknight vegetarian meal.

commentBody: Absolutely delicious! author: Eric Kim
commentBody: One hint about flipping the jeon I make vegetable jeon fairly regularly, and I make them pan-sized (the way they were made at my favorite, long-departed East Village Korean restaurant, Jeollado). When the first side is cooked, I slide the jeon onto a plate, uncooked side up, and then turn the skillet upside-down on top of the plate, and turn the whole thing over using a kitchen towel or 2 oven mitts, Works best with lightweight melamine plates (or Corelle if you're fancy).

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I suspect that Eric is thinking of cuttlefish when he mentions "squid". (Squids vary greatly in size, some are the size of your pinky. while the biggest can snack on an adult human.) Cuttlefish - with proportionately shorter limbs/tentacles -  tastes much meatier than squid, especially after dehydration.
Why discard the ink sac? There are any number of Iron Chef episodes where the umami-rich ink is used to color/flavor diverse foods, including pasta and risotto.

commentBody:
As this is not a traditional jeon, I'll ask 
[1] Why not use Korean pancake mix instead of cornstarch? 
[2] Since twice-cooking is one of the keys to delicious french fries, could a quick par-fry or even microwave of the matchsticks add anything? 
[3] Has anyone tried using a Japanese turning slicer to make fine threads of potato, carrot and/or daikon like the garnishes served with sushi?

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Reading the recipe, I thought "Wow! A total cooking time of 4 to 6 minutes? Starting with raw potatoes (which are really tough to cook through and crisp without burning)? And an egg slurry (which will burn pretty quickly)? And so much corn starch that it's sure to impart an odd flavor...and 2 tablespoons of oil in a 10-12 inch pan?! That's never, ever going to achieve the results in the photo!" Followed the instructions exactly. Odd-tasting, slightly burnt, barley crisp, chewy inside. Not good.

commentBody: Delicious! commentBody: These were great, my friends loved them! I doubled the recipe, to make it work a little better for me, I removed the veggies from my pan before adding the meat, then added it back in once it had seared a bit. Lastly, I made sure to keep the dough covered in the shaping phase so it didn’t dry out since it took so long with so many dumplings.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
You don’t need the flour to hold them together. Instead, I added some (already steamed) diced potatoes, the entire bunch of green onions, and a pinch of paprika and onion powder. Delicious! 
For those asking about cottage cheese subs- it’s a value-add for protein and flavor, and it gives a really nice texture, but you don’t need it - just think of frittatas or Spanish tortillas. This is super flexible and can be played around with as you please. You may want to try adding some milk, though, for texture.

commentBody: This recipe is similar to the spinach-ricotta "pie" I make in order to have calcium and green veg in my diet. It's a great breakfast, lunch or dinner side dish. Bake at 350 for about 45 min to an hour until set. Cut into squares and enjoy!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Revolutionary, I know. I made this exactly as written, and it is fantastic! It took a bit longer to bake, and as soon as the tops were slightly golden I knew they were set to the consistency I prefer. Thank you Eric Kim, for helping me break the carb heavy breakfast habit I acquired during the holidays. 24-7 cookies, anyone?!
RE timing. I know myself well enough to accept that every recipe is going to take me longer than the published time included in most online recipes, especially the first time I prepare something. This is not specific to the NYT. I adjust, allowing for what works for me, in order to create something nourishing for the people I care for.

 In a world full of big concerns, this is the smallest of the small stuff. I suggest taking a breath and remembering why we love to cook. If time is short, there are always alternatives. No need to stress, at least about this.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I made 2 batches for my morning coffee group.  One was made exactly as per the recipe.  For the second batch, before following the recipe, I started by sautéing some finely chopped onion, then added finely chopped fresh spinach, carrots and zucchini, which I had on hand in the fridge.  I seasoned with a little salt, pepper, and Herbs de Provence.  Hands down, the second was the group (and my) favorite - much more flavorful.   I make them now in a 9”x 13” pan, cut into squares (life is too short to hassle with getting them out of cupcake tins) and freeze.  I microwave one or two squares in the morning for breakfast (covering with a damp paper towel to prevent drying) - delicious!!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: For those like me who want the protein but are put off by the extra gunk that is often found in store-bought cottage cheese, I've switched to extra smooth, full fat (10%) ricotta in recipes  (such as ricotta pancakes).  I am VERY pleased with the results. If calories, grams of fat, and grams of protein are a concern, given the quantity of ricotta that would be used here, each "bite" would be similar to using 2% cottage cheese,  BUT 1/10th of the sodium (!)  no chemical stabilizers and delicious.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Re non-dairy cottage cheese substitutes, you can mash some cooked beans such as navy beans or chickpeas. Add non-dairy milk to bind if necessary. 

If you sub vegetables like kale or collard greens for spinach, you can use tofu in place of cottage cheese.  Kale and collard greens are low in oxalates, which are high in spinach. Oxalates combined with the calcium in tofu (if consumed in large amount and consistently) can increase the risk for kidney stones. So not the healthiest combination.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
These are great! I love the Starbucks egg bites, but these are even more nutrient-packed. One minor adjustment:  I just can't do big strands of stringy cooked spinach, even if it is chopped. It's a texture thing. So next time I will squeeze the spinach, then chop the spinach finely, then squeeze again. Otherwise perfect!

To address another concern:  Take the spinach out of the freezer the night before and let it thaw in the sink. Cuts out the microwaving, hand-burning time.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Commenting AGAIN because I’ve been making this recipe every week for the past month, it’s so good! Perfect for the dreaded breakfast meal prep; high protein, easy to transport, good hot and cold. Endlessly riffable too, I think - this week’s has sliced tomatoes on top, and lots of chopped basil.  

I use chickpea flour instead of AP + a very generous amount of nutmeg, let it hydrate for 30 mins in the fridge before baking. Key: don’t bother with muffin tins because it’s actually amazing baked in a lined 9” cake tin, cooled, and cut into 12 slices. Wrap it in the fridge on a plate and it’s perfect for the week!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I used fresh spinach cooked in canola oil.  I drained it in a sieve pressing down to get the extra moisture out. Samin Nostat recommends vegetable spray in her new cookbook Good Things, which I had stopped using but bought some and used that in the cupcake tin. It worked nicely, no sticking. I mixed the eggs before adding them to the bowl with the other ingredients. I added a little Aleppo pepper flakes to give them a little heat. I found the Parmesan not to be a strong enough cheese and would use Gruyère or feta next time. I live in Colorado and found I needed to cook them a little over 30 minutes to get them to brown slightly.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I did make some modifications.  We try to limit our carbs, so I used 5 TBS almond flour and just 1 TBS all-purpose flour (just to act as a binder) along with the baking powder and they turned out great.  I also chopped 3 strips of thick cut bacon, cooked them till crisp, drained and let them cool before adding them to the mix.  I also used feta cheese instead of parmesan because I happened to have it on hand and I love spinach with feta.  Great quick breakfast to pull out of the freezer.

commentBody: I was disappointed in this recipe. Like others, I did not know that “cook time” means the whole time from Step 1 to finish. Further, when I filled each of the 12 muffin cups with 1/3 cup of the mixture, as directed, there was almost half of the mixture left, so I just kept adding more to each of the 12. (Did the recipe misstate?) Also, 20-25 minutes was not long enough to get these done, &,finally, they tasted very blah! As I said…disappointing for a NYT recipe.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: There’s some variability in frozen spinach. Some of it can be a bit stringy with harder stems so unless it seems particularly soft you can achieve a nicer texture if you cook the spinach in it juices and then let them boil down until it’s nearly dry. Then you get the flavor more fully. It will cool while you’re prepping the other ingredients adding dill and feta cheese makes it quite like the filling of spanakopita.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I make these all the time in silicone muffin containers, eat a few and freeze the rest. One egg for two muffins. Beat the eggs and add in any cream, herbs, spices you want, as well as creamy cheeses if using. I love adding chevre as it gives them a nice tang. Fill the muffin cups with fresh or sauteed veggies and grated cheeses and pour the egg mixture over top. Bake at 350 for 20-25 minutes. They don't stick to the silicone cups, probably because I usually saute the veggies and some butter.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Pour into well greased deep pie dish.
Bake at 375°f. Test with a knife at 40 min, if slightly runny bake another 5 min. 
The result was tasty but next time:
- Would put glass pan on baking sheet so ‘crust’ would be less brown.
- Go lighter or omit salt since cheese and Zhoug have salt - was a touch salty but serving with a dollop of plain Greek yogurt mitigated the salt.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: For anyone with their knickers in a twist over these taking more than 5 minutes to prep, I'm here to tell you that it's REALLY not that much more than that. First of all, you're just *thawing* the spinach, not cooking it, so 5 minutes in the microwave is all you need. It'll be just warm enough to not make your hands cold when squeezing it. And LO, in the time it took for your spinach to not be frozen, you've just prepped all your other ingredients (all 4 of them), so now just stir. You're done.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: For all the comments about the worry of using cottage cheese, not to worry. It won't be clumpy or bland. Once blended in the food processor, CC becomes smooth and creamy. It's a high-protein, low calorie addition to dozens of recipes. Because of its neutral taste, it blends well with primary flavors (spinach, garlic, olive oil). I've used it in blueberry pancakes (with a bit of added flour), and even in eggplant dip (baba ganoush). It's creamy and invisible.

commentBody: A good solid recipe. I used square 2 x 2 silicone molds and portioned 3.5 ounces of batter per egg bite to give 12 portions. They came out perfectly without having to grease the pans. Lastly, the is one of those recipes where you need to go heavy on the seasonings.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Thanks to all the helpful comments I tweaked the recipe as below for a delicious result:
- boiled spinach in its juices on the stove for water to evaporate (took about 10mins and avoids slimy texture which I’ve previously had with frozen spinach). Stir regularly. 
- added a chopped garlic clove to spinach mix towards end of above cooking
- added a dollop of hot sauce and a generous handful of chopped fresh mint to overall egg mix to liven up taste
- cooked on 170C for 25mins using patty cake holders in metal muffin pan to avoid removal issues. 
Ate two straight out of the oven (yum) and looking forward to seeing how they hold up tomorrow!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I made these today (18/01/2026) with the following modifications:
I used 600 g frozen creamed spinach
I added 1 tsp garlic powder
I used spelt flour (Dinkel)
I added 2T apple cider vinegar to support the levening agents 
I used 1 tsp baking powder and 1 tsp baking soda.
Cottage cheese was hard to find, so I used Frischkäse

They were delicious

commentBody:
I made these as written.  (I agree that the prep time throws me off because many other recipes consider prep as the stuff we do before we apply the heat such as chopping and mixing, but let’s move on).  They were chunky, but I might revert to pulsing the egg and cottage cheese a few times in a blender to make them smoother.   

Overall, more of a weekend meal prep thing but a nice option to have and will be a great work breakfast.  I did appreciate the large amount of spinach.  Too many of these types of recipes short us on the veg!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This is a very nice recipe which I'd like to experiment with, substituting ricotta for the cottage cheese, for example. I also wouldn't recommend using olive oil to grease the muffin tins. It makes it very difficult to clean later. I used Pam for a smaller batch and cleaning was a breeze. 
  In most modern recipes, “prep time” means actively preparing the ingredients so they are ready to cook — not just gathering them.
Here’s how it’s usually understood. Prep time typically covers: washing produce, peeling, trimming, chopping, slicing, dicing, etc. In other words, when prep time is finished, everything should be ready to go straight into the pan, oven, or pot.  Again, thank you for the recipe.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Went off piste with this recipe and treated it like a @SamSifton ‘no recipe, recipe’ - this quiche is adaptable, use what’s on hand!

Preheat (Propane) oven to 375°f.
Grease a deep glass pie dish (9”x9” or rectangular pan would work too, depends on desired final presentation/serving = not a fan of greasing or washing muffin tins!).

In medium bowl whisk:
6 eggs
A sprinkle Polish pepper/celery/dill salt
A sprinkle nutritional yeast
About 1/8 tsp nutmeg

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I have made egg bites with cottage cheese many time, and I was excited to try this recipe. But it’s so-so. Unlike many of the home cooks who shared comments, mine didn’t stick in the muffin pan, and I’m guessing that’s because I ran a knife around the edge of each one as soon as the pan came out of the oven, and then cooled the pan on a rack. 

However, these egg bites are bland! I used Trader Joe’s low-fat cottage cheese and feta, and it’s funny: at one point I thought about omitting the nutmeg and adding a tablespoon of harissa paste to the mixture, and I’m wishing I had! As egg bites are part of my breakfast food rotation, I will make these again, and add that harissa! Or sub in shallots for the scallions and add garlic powder and more salt. I’m also curious if anyone has tried doubling the accent cheese, as 1/4 cup seemed low. I also saw a comment about sun-dried tomatoes…The opportunity to improve these is great, and for now I’ll eat these with a little salsa on the side.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: These are delicious! I did have to make some changes, which I always detest when reviews write that because as you realize that they changed the whole recipe so it was not a review of the recipe. I promise, mine is. First, I wanted to use whole milk cottage cheese and the grocery store did not have it. So I used Ricotta Cheese, the Italian version of cottage cheese, instead. Then realized that the person who did the grocery shopping only purchased one bag of spinach. So, substituted bacon for the missing spinach. Honestly, I was going to add bacon instead because I am of the philosophy that almost anything is better with bacon. I used keto flour instead of regular flour. One more thing, I did not have any scallions so I used half of a shallot. Finally, I discovered after I had it all mixed that I no longer have large muffin tins. When I make muffins or cupcakes I always bake minis. So, I used the mini muffin tins. They turned out perfect! They also would make a lovely hors d’oeuvre.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: The spinach egg bites are in the oven as I type this.  I followed the directions, including all ingredients.  Perhaps my muffin tin is smaller than others, but there was enough of the mixture to make 14 bites, not the 12 indicated by the recipe.  Ovens differ, but the actual bake time for me was more like 35 minutes.  I let them cool, then tried a bite.  I found it very bland.  I believe next time I make these it will be with sharp cheddar and maybe a dash of Tabasco or cayenne pepper.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This recipe was perfection and the best riff (or update?) on Marian Burros’s plum torte that I’ve ever had. I think it’s the brilliant addition of the whipped cream to the batter, which I’ve never done before (it’s not in her recipe). The next time I make plum torte, I think I’ll add whipped cream. Frankly, I might just use this recipe instead of plum torte as my base for fruit tortes from now on. 

A few things: if I’m juicing a lemon, I’m using the rind. I massaged this into the sugar for the batter. By the time I cored and peeled the persimmons, I needed another half persimmon (so 4.5 persimmons) to have 16 ounces of fruit. Baking it reduces the water content and volume. Next time, I think I’ll use 24 ounces of uncooked fruit. I also think Demerara sugar for the topping might be nice, though the flavor may compete a bit with the cardamom. We shall see! 

Thank you, Eric Kim, for this brilliant recipe that took five years to develop. The world is a better place.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I see the recipe clearly calls for Fuyu persimmons. Please forgive me if I missed this information in the article, but I wonder if this is explained more fully: one MUST use Fuyu persimmons. Some casual readers may not be aware that the two main types of persimmons are Fuyu (non-astringent) and Hachiya (astringent). When exposed to higher temperatures, like baking, Hachiya persimmons become even more astringent; using Hachiya persimmons would produce a very bad tasting dessert.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I’m a persimmon expert at this point - I have a huge Hachiya tree & access to free Fuyu persimmons. I’ve made dozens of persimmon cakes, breads, cookies etc. I found that 1lb of Fuyu cut in 8 wedges each did not cover the cake enough nor did it give huge persimmon flavor. I ended up cutting them thinner once I realized there were too few. Also this cake could benefit from Hachiya persimmon pulp in the batter. 
The coconut extract was hardly detectable :-(
It was also unclear why to put a sheet of parchment in a sling style vs a parchment round if instructions say to serve in the pan?
And from my husband: whipped cream for serving needs sugar or it tastes flacid. That’s all!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I went to the store specifically get cardamom for this recipe. I went through the whole recipe until I got to the sugar topping, at which point I realized I’d bought coriander, not cardamom. After mild self-flagellation, I baked it (sugar on top only), and chat—it’s delicious. I don’t know if this is a happy accident or a near miss, but it’s really good. There is a je ne se quoi about it that makes it addicting. Eventually I’ll try this with cardamom but I would make it again with coriander.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I enjoyed making this cake.  It reminded me of a favorite German cake that I like to make - anpfelkuchen (although I think the German palate might find this a bit spicy, luckily I am half Korean and can handle some spice). I also love idea of making this with seasonal fruits - and persimmon always makes me think of Christmas.  So Apple for fall, persimmon for winter - maybe cherries for summer?  Will need a spring fruit to test out in 2026.

Because the apfelkuchen recipe I have calls for a springform pan, I used what I had and it looked beautiful when removed from the pan.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I made this as given (except I had no coconut extract, so substituted vanilla instead) and it was/is delicious.   The timing was just right for roasting the persimmon, the the timing for baking the cake “about an hour” was way off — the toothpick came out clean at 30 minutes perfectly dry. I let it go another five minutes and pulled it out. I used a newly acquired Breville air fryer oven pro (default is convection), but it’s never been wrong baking pies and biscuits in the past.  One hour would have over-baked my persimmon cake.

commentBody: We have 2 Fuyu trees that have given us hundreds of fruit this year.  I’ve sent ten medium size priority usps boxes to family and friends.  Small Fuyus easily weigh 4 ounces. Large ones over 8 ounces.  In store prices vary with size, from $3 a pound for small fruit to $7 for large organic.  Many people have no experience with Fuyus, and confuse the with acorn shaped Hachiyas which are astringent and inedible until super ripe.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I wish I had read comments before I baked. I’ve made this twice now and first time had it in the oven for an hour and it was over done. Second time I took it out after 45 minutes and it was still a little over done. I also cut the sugar the second time. I don’t understand the comments about the cake being dry since even the one that stayed in the oven for the full hour was moist. What I don’t get is the pic showing a light-colored cake on the inside. Both of mine were much darker. Also, didn’t use parchment paper to line pan and it was fine. Anyway, love this cake! I’ve run out of persimmons for the season but will reuse this recipe when my persimmon tree bears fruit again next fall.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Used frozen Hachiya puree because it's what I had and the third time was the charm. Added 1/4 cup lightly sweetened puree to batter and folded in all whipped cream. Poured half of batter into prepped springform and browned at 425 degrees. Then spread puree in layer on top of browned cake and poured the rest of batter on top. Puree jelled and remained as a layer. Will make it this way again but will make more batter.

I’m currently trying to hoard any remaining persimmons in stores so I can freeze them and continue to bake this until they’re in season again!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Terrific flavor.  
I'd already decided for next time to cut persimmons much smaller and scatter into the dough, with a few on top for aesthetics; just now I noticed that the photo shows them shot right through the middle of the cake. Nothing can be wrong here.
Since cardamom and ginger have become my favorite spices--the more the better--on vegetables, I added a little ginger too. Personally, I wouldn't mind more of all the spices for this cake though that might be gilding the lily for some.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: What a clever idea. My husband and I enjoyed this cake. I took my persimmons out of the oven after 25 minutes, and the cake after 45. I also did as others suggested and reduced the sugar in the batter to 150g. The cardamom, while it wasn't overpowering, was still the dominant flavor for me, but I wonder if the cake would have tasted a bit plain without it. Thanks, Eric, for coming up with a recipe for a persimmon cake!

commentBody:
Excellent cake that is better after a "sit" of a couple of days to let the flavors marry.  Wrapped it tight in plastic and set in the refrigerator for a couple of days - holds up quite well.

We also found we liked this better with quince. BUT, that is quince that we had previously cooked and jarred.  Fine with persimmon, better with quince.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Sliced 5 Fuyus into sixteenths, baked full time; they lose flavor. Reduced sugar to 150g and replaced 20% of cream with good eggnog. Baked full time, looked terrific, let it sit wrapped for a day. Turned out dry. Check doneness at 30 mins, esp if reducing sugar. Fruit flavor barely detectable. Would try again with full sugar or 150g brown sugar, and repeat the eggnog whip. Was excited to try persimmons in baking but perhaps they're best enjoyed raw to retain their delicate crunch and scent.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Made twice in a week.  First time, as per recipe, but 3 persimmons weighed a lb., and 8” pan.  Looked just like pic, but was drier than i would have liked.  Second time, used  9” springform, and increased cinnamon and cardamom in dough each by 1/2 tsp.  Cake didn’t rise enough to cover fruit this time, thought i had a fail, but it was moister and even more flavorful.  Maybe persimmons were a bit riper cause more difficult to cut, etc, but i think they tasted better.  In summary, not sure why it didn’t rise as much, but loved the cake.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
RE: Using dried chickpeas: even with overnight-soaked chickpeas, you'll want to use a pressure cooker/instant pot, on the "beans" setting (35 mins pressure + natural release). Cooking beans/chickpeas without pressure will take thrice as long. 
In South Asia, where legumes are the major daily source of dietary protein, a pressure cooker has been an essential kitchen device for at least 80 years. Urban life is too busy to spend 90+ minutes on a single recipe.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This recipe's similar to the South Asian dish cholé  (="chickpeas" in Punjabi), but uses yoghurt as a souring agent instead of tamarind, unripe mango powder (aamchoor), or dried pomegranate seeds. (Most cholé  recipes also use proportionately more tomatoes, plus fresh ginger and garam masala) . 
I'm wondering why Eric calls for draining the canned chickpeas only to add 2 cups of water in step 3. The canning liquid (known as "aquafaba" to vegans) is perfectly palatable.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
For the canned chickpea arguments:
1. yes from scratch is easy and it tastes better.
2. cans do often have calcium disodium EDTA as a preservative.
3. To boot, the cans are typically lined with endocrine disrupting chemicals. It may say it's BPA free but do you know if they just replaced it with a chemical sibling called BPS?? (yes it is just as bad for you, but few consumers know about it yet).

Just soak the beans and make your own. They're cheaper, better and easy.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: For those using dried chickpeas (chana or chole in Hindi), here's an easy hack. Submerge the beans in boiling water, cover with a lid, and soak for as long as possible. Even an hour or two will soften them, but longer is better. Then pressure-cook for approximately 25-35 min until they're fully cooked. A smidge--and I mean a smidge-- of baking soda in the pressure cooker will quicken the cooking process. But be careful--too much baking soda and the chickpeas will be mush.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
@Edith:"The liquid from the canned chickpeas is full of sodium and preservatives." 
Please read the can's label.
Most brands use only salt (i.e., sodium chloride, the "sodium" referred to). Whether you use the (mildly salted) liquid or added salt, it's the same molecule. 
Some add antibacterial sorbic acid - originally isolated from rowanberries - see Wikipedia.
I use dry chickpeas+Instant Pot simply because with canned chickpeas, you're mostly paying for water (+ convenience).

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Adding to the chickpea discussion:

  1.  in my experience, a 15 oz. can is 1.5 cups drained; so 3 cans is 4.5 cups.  In the Tip on using dry beans, it says use 2 cups of dry--which in my experience = 6 cups cooked.   I like chickpeas, so don't care, but directions are off by a lot.

2.  When using dry beans instead of canned, the standard equivalency is to cook the dried beans, so they are added at the same point in the recipe as the canned.  That's what I'd do here.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This sounds delicious.
Since someone recommended pressure cooking, here's my two cents: my mother uses a pressure cooker, but I don't.  The advantage of shorter cooking time is not that great when cooking most modern legumes you buy at the supermarket.  What does make it longer to cook is the addition of acidic ingredients. One disadvantage with the pressure cooker is that you can't check the progress of whatever is being cooked, or add ingredients in the middle.

I do wonder what the recipe was like before it was adapted by Eric Kim. V

commentBody: For something so very chickpea forward, this was very good. I made a few little tweaks based on what I had. No chilis but I added a healthy spoonful of dried aleppo pepper. author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Outstanding recipe, and as someone else mentioned, it is reminiscent of (channa) punjabi.

I strained but did not rinse the (canned) chickpeas, but that is just my preference, and I used minced garlic in a tube rather than a microplane for staging the garlic into the yogurt.  I also used a 13.5oz can of diced tomatoes rather than using a fresh tomato.

I followed the recipe at it was showcased otherwise, and my wife and I were both thrilled!  We're definitely going to make this one again.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Disappointed with this. At first. It was okay, but never thickened. And whoever said 10 minutes for prep time either had an army of people helping or had been drinking a lot of coffee.

Changes that I made: added two large chopped garlic cloves to the onions. 

This seems to be one of those dishes that gets better once it sits for a day or two. I made it on Sunday for a potluck and got lukewarm comments on it. I had the leftovers for lunch on Tuesday. The flavor was much much better.

commentBody: This was delicious.  Made it with dried chickpeas.  The 2 cups recommended seemed like a lot and used 1 cup (1/2 lb) and same amounts of everything else (plus additional liquid). commentBody:
I made this during the recent snow storm, substituting yellow onion instead of red, and dried chile flakes instead of serrano, and it was fabulous. Very tasy and satisfying. commentBody: This was one of the better NYT recipes I've made lately and I'll make it again. It's not hard to prepare dried chick peas: use 1 cup, bring to a boil covered with 2" of water after rinsing, boil 5 minutes then turn off the heat and let them soak with the pot lid on for an hour. Drain, add 1/2 tsp of baking soda, cover with fresh water and simmer until tender (about 45 minutes). Add 1 tsp salt and drain chick peas just before adding to stew. I subbed 1/2 c canned tomatoes for fresh and used two Thai chiles. Also added 3 leaves of finely chopped lacinato kale with the spices. Replace your ground spices once a year or grind them fresh in a mortar if you find that the recommended amounts resulted in a bland dish.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I've never understood how you "very finely chop" a tomato--or chop it into any other size of dice, for that matter. Even when I carefully cut a tomato apart with a sharp serrated knife, the tomato turns into a giant blob of mush. Seeds and gel squirt everywhere, and any remaining firm parts are nearly impossible to chop. It's so frustrating that if a can of small-dice tomatoes (or just tomato puree) from the grocery store isn't a workable addition, I skip the recipe altogether.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I made these to add to our Christmas treat freezer, and as I distributed the cut-off ends to some hovering family members the verdict was unanimous: this one is a keeper. 

As a former pastry chef, I felt confident to make three small changes to the recipe. First, I used a 9x12 pan because I wanted a few more bars to tuck away. This did not impact the cooking time, in fact I found the recipe needed longer than called for to achieve the lightly golden brown finish, despite using a larger pan. I ended up with a generous 3/4” cookie base. 

I used a whole egg instead of the egg white because I’m too lazy to separate an egg and storing one yolk is annoying. This resulted in perhaps a slightly chewier base that we all loved. 
Lastly, I added 3/4 cup mini chocolate chips to the cooking base. A recipe for mint chocolate chips cookies leads me to expect chocolate chips, so I took the liberty and it was a good idea. 

A note of caution: upon adding the peppermint extract, the melted white chocolate immediately seized. To correct I added 1 tablespoon of boiling water and gave it a whisk and it came back together perfectly.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Shout out to @K.C.H. for the helpful tips. My chocolate did seize and I was able to smooth it out with 1 Tbsp of boiling water. 

Tip for the top delaminating from the bottom: when I begin to trim the edges from the whole pan right out of the fridge, I noticed that  the chocolate was delaminating from those edge pieces. I left the whole pan  to come to room temperature and was then able to cut into triangles without any further delaminating.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Just made these and they are delicious. Personally, I'd like them a teeny bit mintier, if you really like mint I suggest another 1/8-1/4 tsp extract in either dough or chocolate, probably not bother. I had an 8x8 metal pan and expected to need an additional 5-10 min but it was done perfectly at 30 min. My white chocolate chips were old and I think I used a bad bowl type (corelle) and my chips burned, never melted. Ended up switching to melting wafers in a bowl over simmering water, the switch in chocolate made me need 3x the food colors (6 blue, 3 yellow) for the perfect color. The wafers set much faster, but also chip a bit more when cut.

commentBody: These were an unexpected delight all around. They were chewy and soft and the flavor was wonderful. I was really happy this recipe turned out as nice as it sounded. My dad died of cancer last November and I had a habit of making him cookies during his illness to try to fatten him up a bit and boost his spirits. Right before he died I asked him what sort of cookies he'd like and he said 'mint chocolate chip'. Alas, he never got to try these but it warmed my soul to make them in his honor.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Wonderful recipe, and thanks to the helpful comments, this came out beautifully for me as a very unskilled baker! I used Tollhouse chips, and they seized after adding the peppermint (I had to google what that meant). I added another 1/2 tsp of olive oil, but it was the tablespoon of boiling water that fixed it. To be fair, I also did not measure the chips. My kitchen is around 63 degrees. So I only cooled the cookie on the counter before adding the frosting. Then, I let it cool on the counter for another 1.5 hrs before cutting it up. The chocolate did not delaminate. I also cooked it in a 9x13, but it didn’t go all 13 inches, more like 11. This really is stunning and refreshing. Will make again!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I made these today after watching the video and reading a lot of comments. I was able to successfully cut them up and didn’t lose any topping! Here’s what I did: First, because I only had an 8x8” pan, my cookie base was a little thicker, so I baked it for 30 minutes and figured that would be slightly underbaked if anything. I cooled the base for only about 20-25 minutes before adding the white chocolate mixture and topping with the dark chocolate. Then I let the whole thing cool on a windowsill in my (fairly cold, probably about 62-64°) kitchen. A few hours later I cut it up and it held together perfectly. One caveat: the dark chocolate shavings melted on the warm base, but they still look great.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Delicious, not too minty, even tastier day 2. Easy but requires attention to sequencing and timing. 1 whole egg worked well. Painted white chocolate to the edge, did not trim crispy edges, no one was sad. Had to bake for 30 min in a 9x9 pan, cooled on counter 1 hour, then was sure not to overheat the white chocolate (left a few lumps that can be stirred until smooth), then added olive oil, then mint (no seizing). Chilled a little more than 15 min., cut little squares w/ no separation issue.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I made a double batch of these, determined to avoid delamination. I’m happy to say that two little adjustments led to success. First, I followed a comment to drag a fork across the surface of the dough prior to cooking. This resulted in a cookie with more area for the topping to adhere to. My second change involved timing. Rather than refrigerating the chocolate-topped product before cutting, I allowed the chocolate topping to cool at room temp for ~an hour, then made my cuts. Success and the neighbors loved them!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
OMG I just made these and I absolutely love them.  Here are a few things I did to help me after reading other people's comments and Eric's responses to them:
I decided to use a 10x10 so I could get more
I used a whole egg
Dough was soft so put little flour on my hand to spread
After melting the chocolate, I added the oil, and then gradually added the peppermint
Press bittersweet gently into white choc to adhere
In the fridge 5 mins, took out and started cutting.  Chocolate was 80 - 90 % hard.

The part I struggled with the most was spreading the dough into the pan in an even layer since I didn't have a little metal spatula like Eric did in the video. The dough kept sticking to my rubber spatula. I ended up folding over the sides of the parchment paper on top of the dough and using the rubber spatula to get it even between the layers of parchment. Worked beautifully!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Fun, cute and tasty, but we had the same problem others have mentioned: the white chocolate layer did not adhere to the shortbread, falling off when we cut the cookies out. The emergency fix: we ran the shortbread cookies under the broiler for about 60 seconds, then pushed the white chocolate tops down firmly onto the shortbread. They melted enough to re-adhere to the shortbread. Saved!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: This is a beautiful cookie and Eric Kim is brilliant. But ... it's a special occasion cookie. I want an every-day, less fussy version. So I adapted it like this: make the dough as written, but use 2 tsp peppermint extract. Stir in 4 oz (= 3/4 cup) white chocolate chips and 2 oz (= scant 1/2 cup) dark chocolate chips. Bake as directed, cool as directed, and cut into squares. Similar flavor profile to the recipe, less pretty presentation-- but fast and easy to make.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I made these to take to an event; they got rave reviews (I loved them too!). The chocolate layer stayed on: I put in fridge for about 15 minutes, then covered by a towel on the counter overnight before cutting in the morning. One problem though: the dough was super sticky and hard to spread evenly, so the thicknesses varied widely. I tried spreading with offset spatulas - both metal and plastic; then put flour on the spatulas; then tried with powdered sugar on the spatulas. The dough still stuck to each iteration. I measured the dry ingredients with a scale so can’t pin the blame there. Any suggestions?

commentBody: Keep the dough recipe as is, except do a 9x12 pan because this cookie needs a thinner base. Then just double the recipe for the top half of the cookie. Then I think it's improved. commentBody: I made these twice this week, using store-bought egg whites. The first time I overestimated the amount of white from "1 lg egg", and they came out chewier. Still delicious! During batch 2 I got frustrated trying to even out the batter in the pan so I threw a piece of parchment on top and smoothed it with my hand. There may be a reason not to do that, but it seemed to work nicely! These are crowd pleasers and are going into my top 5 Christmas cookie list. Thanks Eric!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Came here to rave about my new favorite cookie recipe & their genius developer. (I make last year’s lemon turmeric crinkles end to end in December and can confirm they are great for breakfast). This one’s perfection in taste and texture. I cut off the amber/shard thin edges and they would be brilliant cookies on their own, I’ll be playing around with that idea. ( +fat European butter doesn’t hurt). Anyhow, I made it exactly as written and was wondering why my lovely green chocolate top sheered clean off after I sliced (very fun btw). Until seeing some comments and realizing I’d spaced on the olive oil. If anyone’s wondering, it seems it has a function. I have gorgeous chewy butter cookies with a minty chocolate hat/chaser. Don’t care. They’re still stupendous. Eric I love so many of your creative, fun, novel recipes, especially your homey takes on Korean mainstays.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I successfully made these dairy-free as, unfortunately, I am ultra-dairy intolerant. Using an Earth Balance stick and white chocolate chips from Chocolate Emporium in Cleveland, I had no problems at all and they are delicious. I baked 28 min (very light in color), cooled to room temp, my icing was perfectly spreadable, and I had it in the fridge for about fifteen minutes and then cut. They were the perfect texture (a little chewy and a little bite) and the chocolate icing stayed on PERFECTLY! Thank you NYT Cookie Week. I now have more unique and stunning cookies than days to cook them! Eric Kim, all of your cookies are my favorite!!!!!!!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I tried to spread the dough into a 9x13 pan as I didn’t haven’t 9 square, and it was a disaster. It stuck horribly to the parchment paper I used on top to spread it out. Don’t do that. I then scraped all the dough off the parchment and into an 8 square pan and spread it as best I could with a spatula. Once warm in the oven, the dough melted into a lovely evenly distributed layer. All that worry for nothing! Anyway, these cookies are fantastic. The texture is wonderful. I did use the entire egg. I can’t wait to make another batch for Christmas as there’s no way this batch will make it til then.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Made a double batch on the weekend-  the minty quality of the short bread  was delightful. used gel colors for a wonderful minty green. Seeing that others had issues with delaminating makes me feel not quite so bad -- as that's the only problem. Maybe I cooked the base too long,  "room temp"is a relative term- so maybe too cold when the green went on. They taste terrific, I'm just telling folks that they come with a removable top.  Will aim to be more precise next time. thx

commentBody: These cookies are absolutely delicious! One of the best mint chocolate desserts I've had. Here was the only issue. I stored them in the fridge uncut for 2 days. My baking coach ChatGPT told me that you never cut white chocolate cold. I should have cut them at room temperature -- next time.

commentBody: I made this twice.  The first time I did the manual method which was no fun for my 70+ year old shoulder.  I have a kitchen stocked with lots of great ELECTRIC appliances so the 2nd time I used the stand mixer and paddle attachment for all mixing and it worked just fine.  I creamed the butter first, then added the confectioners sugar on stir. I did have issues with delamination of the topping even after letting it come back to room temperature.  The flavor is great.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I really wanted to love these. Sadly, it was a swing and a miss for me. It is totally this was a me problem, but the cookie base ended up being EXTREMELY chewy, like had to gnaw on it chewy. Maybe I over mixed the dough? (I put in the sweat and did it by hand!!) Maybe I overbaked them? I really don’t know. I was baking at a friend’s house and she didn’t have a food scale, so I had to use the fluff method; maybe I used too much flour. When it came time for the white chips, I had to approximate that it was a half cup, but then there was WAY too much extract, and I had to add more white chocolate. Even the food coloring didn’t work right. I just threw the rest of them away because I kept gnawing on them and being disappointed. Sigh!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Delicious! Details: 1) Williams Sonoma Goldtouch Pro 9x9" pan; 2) used hand-held silicone bowl scraper as a spatula (for good control); 3) Used WFM peppermint in oil (not alcohol); 4) used WFM food coloring (4 drops spirulina blue: 2 drops turmeric yellow); 5) chipped the dark chocolate with a just-sharpened knife and kept cool (NOTE - aim for thicker chips next time); 6) let the spread chocolate topping set for 1h at room temp, then sliced, THEN refrigerated ... topping stayed intact - perfect!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Made these for my 83 year old FIL who loves mint chocolate chip ice cream.  They are not a simple cookie to make, but MAN, are they delicious!  This recipe is a keeper.  I baked them this morning, and just cut them.  They are beautiful!  My entire family is asking for more and I'm having to fend them off to ensure my father-in-law gets some!

I used a whole egg based on others comments, and used my Kitchen Aid rather than do by hand. Other than that, made exactly to recipe.  Thank you Eric!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Upon cutting, I also had the same “delamination” happen with the white chocolate topping, so I melted some more of the bittersweet chocolate that was used as sprinkle topping, pulled off the white chocolate topping slab, and added the melted bittersweet chocolate kind of like a glue and then put the which chocolate slab back on. So, now there’s the cookie layer, a bittersweet chocolate layer, and on top the white chocolate with sprinkles and crushed candy canes. It still looks pretty and it may resolve the issue of not being able to put it in a cookie box. I also added 2 tablespoons of chocolate to the batter in addition to putting in the whole egg. I plan to make these again.

commentBody: I was so excited to cut into the cookie today….every step went just as smoothly as in the video (true confession, I used a stand mixer for the dough). The cookie cooled for way over an hour….I used Ghirardelli’s white melting wafers. How can I keep this from happening again? I really want to try to do it….it’s a beautiful cookie with great flavor and texture! Hope someone can help!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Granddaughter (12) and I made these last night. Would advise using 9x13 pan to spread out base and make it thinner - as it is, is fairly thick cookie with a very thin layer of icing. Icing does not stick to base - separates as a stand alone shell of candy coating. Requiring mixing sugar and butter by hand seems a little precious - why not use an electric mixer? Having a 12 year old with time on her hands made it do-able, but as a busy cook, I normally wouldn't bother. I rate this a 6/10.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: These cookies are a keeper! I made 2 batches, 1 with bread flour (out of AP) and 1 with King Arthur 1-1 GF flour (subbed by weight, not volume). I think the bread flour turned out a bit chewier, but good. The GF are even better IMO - light, chewy but crisp somehow. I did have a slight delamination issue with the chocolate topping, but I also tried to cut them tiny - I like my cookies bite size for cookie trays! I didn't have 2 9x9 pans, so I used a tart pan & it would make a gorgeous cookie cake

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I made a double batch in a 9 x 13” pan. When I 
cut them, the chocolate started to fall off. I noticed the sharp triangle edges exacerbated the problem. So I cut them into squares and rectangles. The topping adhered to the cookie when cut that way. I think this is a failed cookie. Too many people have complained about it. It’s too finicky. That being said I did like the texture and taste of the cookie but what a waste to cut all of the sides off. Which, by the way, was the best part of the cookie. This one I will not be making again.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I used an 8x8 pan and had to bake about 10-15 mins longer than what the recipe called for.

I was aware of the two issues with this recipe (white chocolate seizing and separating from the cookie). My white chocolate did not seize. After applying the white chocolate spread, I let it sit for about 10 minutes and then put the dark chocolate on. I put it in the fridge for about 20-30 minutes. It wasn’t the coldest it could be but I took it out and cut them into individual cookies and the chocolate did not separate from the cookie. I found I had to put more green food colouring in as the blue I had was really strong.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Just made these and they turned out great. I was a little worried about the issues called out in the comments about the chocolate not sticking (thanks everyone for the tips).

Some things that seemed to work for me:
- I baked the cookie layer until there was barely browning on the edges. The final result was pretty chewy but I prefer that.
- After applying the chocolate layers, I only refrigerated for about 5 minutes, and then took it out and let it set at room temp for 20-30 mins.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Nice recipe! I enjoyed these, although in the future I’d make a few modifications. For one, I think I’d prefer them a bit thinner — I used the whole egg and made a 1.5x batch for a 9x13” tray (roughly the same surface area to volume ratio as the original recipe), but next time I’d just do a normal batch for the 9x13 so they come out a bit thinner.

I think I’d also slightly increase the amount of white chocolate on top, maybe to 1.5-2x, but that’s just because I like the textural contrast.

Last note, on the issue of delamination: I made two batches, one with Valrhona and one with Nestle white chocolate. The Nestle chocolate seized slightly when I added the dyes and extract, while Valrhona didn’t. As it turns out, the Valrhona batch was a bit more prone to delaminating at the edges of cookies. I think that in this case, you want your chocolate to be out of temper. If it is in temper, it will act more like bark and less like icing, and become prone to snapping off. Try using a slightly lower quality chocolate or letting it seize slightly (maybe by adding a drop of water? I didn’t experiment with this) if you want to avoid delamination. Also also, use less dye than the recipe calls for — my first batch was dark blue.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: 5(taste)/3(difficulty). The dough was so sticky. I stepped away from the pan for 15 min. after having the basic square shape - when I came back, it lost the stickiness and was easy to pat flat with my hands. For the white choc. layer, I'd recommend you make at least 2x. It set quickly and I had to scramble and make a second batch to cover where I couldn't get it to spread. I also had about 1/3 of the cookies delaminate. Glued back on w/ extra melted chocolate. Pretty though!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: My dough was super thick, even with following weight directions. I ended up having to push the dough in the pan with my fingers which resulted in a really bumpy top that made spreading the chocolate difficult. I'm not sure where I went wrong- the cookie seemed caught between something that snapped and something that was more chewy. I also made the mistake of putting the chocolate shavings on the white chocolate before it had properly cooled which meant a lot of smaller pieces melted right away!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: These were super easy, and the texture is really nice. I ran out of peppermint extract (alcohol based) and only had peppermint flavor (oil based), so I ended up using 1/2 tsp extract + 1/2 tsp flavor for the base and all flavor for the topping. They were SO pepperminty, almost the level of toothpaste, gum, or breath mints, a bit too much for us. But maybe they are supposed to be this minty? I'm assuming this might have to do with using flavor vs. extract?

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
The cookie base turned out great. I followed comments and used a whole egg rather than separating the white.

When it came to the icing though… everything fell apart. I followed the colouring instructions, and it turned Cookie Monster blue. It did also seize a bit as some comments suggested, so I followed the advice to whisk in a small amount of boiling water. Unfortunately that turned it grainy. And still Cookie Monster. The icing got chucked and I coated the whole thing in a mixture of melted milk and white chocolate (coloured with a drop of green).

Anyway, cookies were mostly salvaged but the icing was a disaster.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: My son loved the look of these, so I made them in lieu of the cocoa cutouts w/ mint buttercream that I usually do for Christmas.  I will not do so again.  Followed recipe as written.  Dough was difficult to spread evenly, white chocolate coating was fussy, and ultimately these just weren't worth the effort. Aside from the insistent mintiness due to the extract in both parts, the components don't really relate to one another: firm mint white chocolate sits on dense, chewy cookie. Meh.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
These have been the crowd favorite from my cookie box this year! 
I have made two batches and both times had the delamination issue with the white chocolate. The second time I used Eric’s tip of pulling them out at 25 min., letting them cool slightly then adding the topping but still had the same issue. I decided to pop off the whole white chocolate layer and made a royal icing instead with the food coloring and peppermint. I cut the bars into rectangles, dipped them in the icing then added the chocolate chunks. The original white chocolate topping added a nice creaminess but the royal icing still tasted great, looked beautiful and stayed on the cookie when I cut it! Next time I make these, if I have the delamination issue again, I may try making a buttercream

commentBody: In the south, they don’t use marshmallows like they do in Illinois or other northern states I have lived in. You can tone down the sugar to your like-ing. All those marshmallows harken back to the 1950s classic recipe.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Perfect without need to broil
Our Pyrex pan supposedly explodes if put it in the broiler, so instead I bumped the heat to 425 degrees (temperature ceiling for newer Pyrex), and waited until I saw the little wisp of smoke.  Marshmallows were perfectly browned a little bit crunchy on top, just like Eric’s picture!  
We’re switching from our traditional streuseled recipe to this one for Thanksgiving.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This is the best sweet potato casserole I’ve had by a mile. The sweet potatoes are delightfully savory and the marshmallows add a nice crunch and sweetness. It is definitely worth seeking out fresh bay leaves as they bring the sweet potatoes to a level that dry bay leaves can’t. 

I used coconut milk instead of cream to make it low lactose. You could taste the coconut in the final dish but it really works with the sweet potato, bay and pepper.  I think this could easily be made vegan by using coconut milk, vegan butter or neutral oil, and vegan marshmallows. 

A new Thanksgiving tradition!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Before this recipe: Eric Kim. Cute kid who does lots of tasty things with Gochujang.
After this recipe: Eric Kim, Sweet Potato genius.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Amazing. I made a day ahead and baked before the meal. Follow the steps -- he's right about cooking the potatoes for what seems like an insanely long time. I cut it short by 15 minutes and ended up using an immersion blender to get the texture he gets in the video. I've never seen fresh bay leaves in my life so I used dried and I added a little fresh sage based on comments here. It was great. As he suggested I baked 5 extra minutes because it was refrigerated. The marshmallows came out nicely browned so I skipped the broiler for fear of messing with it. 

Will definitely make again. I would bake this earlier in the rotation on the day of Thanksgiving because it thickens nicely as it cools.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
My first NYT Cooking comment! Had to cause really loved this dish. Still so close to our traditional sweet potatoes and marshmallows (I used to just mash them and top with marshmallows) but the sage gives it so much depth, without being overly savory. 

And in the kitchen mayhem getting dinner on the table with too many cooks, in an airbnb kitchen, we tried to ruin this dish. First the casserole dish top got left on and the marshmallows melted.  (Who knew marshmallows melted to a clear syrup?) We scraped/drained the syrup left behind and tried again.The new marshmallows burnt in a second. My kids (grown) scraped off the burnt parts and ate it anyway, getting second helpings. If that’s not the sign of a great dish, I don’t know what is.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
"Veganized"
1.  Vegan butter 2.  West Life Organic soy milk with 1.5 tsp of vegan butter for the cream 3.  Super chilled aquafaba whipped to stiff peaks for the eggs.  4.  Vegan Marshmellows.   Did everything else per recipe, except I folded whipped aquafaba into the potato mixture just before putting it into the baking dish plus two additonal cracks of black pepper.  

So yummy even without the marshmallows.  Had to make an additional half batch because we ate most of the first before getting to the oven! [Benefit of vegan cooking..hahahaha]

This is a keeper and our new favorite.  Thanks Eric and NYT Cooking!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: This was so delicious and savory! I don’t think I’ll make sweet potatoes any other way. It was incredibly quick and easy to make too after the potatoes are done roasting. I made this dairy and egg free by swapping the cream with a can of coconut cream. Then I just didn’t add the eggs at all and it turned out delicious. The only tip I have is that 1.5 hrs bake time must be for really huge potatoes. I used a bunch of medium to smaller sized potatoes and they were burnt to a crisp after 1.5 hrs. I tried again and made sure to keep checking and they were fork tender after 35-40 minutes.

commentBody: This was my first time making sweet potato casserole and this recipe will definitely be in the Thanksgiving rotation from now on. The only real change I made was to make a pecan streusel with butter, flour, brown sugar, and chopped pecans instead of the ones called for. I did have to cook it at 375 with another dish due to limited oven space, but the marshmallows came out perfectly toasted and crisp after about 25 minutes with no need to use the broiler.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
We've tried so many sweet potato recipes for Thanksgiving over the years and, with this, we've found our winner!! Love the taste of bay in the sweet potatoes and that they're not too sweet. They were a favorite of everyone at the table--even those who usually don't like sweet potato casserole. 

I made homemade marshmallows with sugar (not corn syrup) for this dish and those were delicious too. Think they could use more marshmallows--I'll do that next year :).

Thank you, Eric!!

commentBody:
I made two batches of this for Thanksgiving and they stole the show!  It had multiple flavor layers.. On one hand it was savory, and on the other hand it was warm and sweet.  Of course the pecans add the perfect crunch.  Absolutely perfect.

The only thing I'll say is that for my second batch, I had to store it for 2 days but had already placed the marshmallows.  I think that should be done right before it gets put in the oven, as they ended up melting.  Only matters if you care about plating.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Totally not mushy peas. Sheesh. 

Start with dried peas
Put em in a big bowl
Add ten cups of water
Add 2 Tablespoons of baking soda 
Soak overnight
Rinse like crazy
Add peas to pot and cover with water
Add salt
Cook til tender - about 20 minutes, stirring gently maybe once. (Scooping out foam.  If they’re boiling out of their shells, turn them down.)

Serve with apple cider vinegar, using the cap as a way to measure. Heaven.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I made this a little more Chicken Parm style:

I had rotisserie chicken leftovers, so I took the pieces, coated them in egg, and dredged them in plain bread crumbs. Then I put them into the air fryer at 400 for 10-12 minutes (tossing/checking every 5min.) — all while the pasta was boiling. 

I didn’t use as much sauce, as I was making a 1/2 recipe, but I kept it somewhat light in sauce anyway. I wanted to preserve the crispy chicken bits. I had some leftover fresh mozz (not as creamy as burrata) to sub in. 

I will cover mine next time as the lighter sauce made it a little dry on top, but everything melted and it was hot through and through. Served sauce on side and that helped. 

All in all it came out great!
