author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Tom Ryan, the founder of the national brand “Smashburger” has a PhD in Taste and Smell Profiles from MIT. The presence of butter when smashing patties onto a hot surface creates the Maillard Reaction, which he lovingly refers to as “meat candy”. A little dab will do and the thinner the smash, the more meat candy you wind up with. A good smashed burger can be best described as “lacy” in texture and it is a completely different game than the thicker, juicier versions you would throw on the grill. Furthermore, the thin patties are meant to be stacked on top of each other. 2-3 patties yields about the same amount of meat as a single “grill” burger would. Little more effort for a lot more flavor. 

I adore all styles of burgers permitting they’re not made of chicken, tofu or impossible meat. I adore smashed burgers the most.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Seared Scallops With Tomato Salad Recipe
commentBody:
My tip: do this on a grill outside. Heat your pan over the grill. Not only will it taste a bit barbecued, the stink will be outside. It's impossible to cook scallops without the stink--I have a range hood that is so powerful it pulls my kitchen doors shut, and it cannot remove the stink as fast as searing scallops creates it.

Also: heat the pan DRY (I use cast iron) and then wipe it quickly with an oil-soaked paper towel or basting brush that is a bit oily

(Same goes for pan-blackened fish...)

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Seared Scallops With Tomato Salad Recipe
commentBody: Frozen scallops are horrible.  Many place defrost frozen scallops, so buy from a quality fish market and buy day boat scallops. Day boats are usually plump and look fresh like any fresh fish. Before you check out, open the package and smell them, if they don’t smell like the sea, leave them in the store. Day boat scallops are running $30 per pound in NJ and are worth it.  I have bought nice stuff at wegmans and livottis.  And occasionally in Shop Rite.  Watch the display cases.

Day boat scallops are running $30 per pound in NJ and are worth it.  I have bought nice stuff at wegmans and livottis.  And occasionally in Shop Rite.  Watch the display cases.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I’m obsessed with this cookie. The recipe is super easy to follow. I got my freeze dried raspberries from Trader Joe's. One bag is the approximate amount that you need for this recipe. This is definitely going to be a cookie recipe I will be using regularly, especially as gifts to my friends. I think this may be better than a chocolate chip cookie in my opinion. In terms of size of the cookie, I basically made them into medium sized balls (about an inch to an inch and a half diameter) and they were the perfect size. Crispy but also beautifully chewy. 10 out of 10!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I just took a batch out of the oven .... mmmm! Using a tablespoon scoop, the recipe made 24 cookies. I put the cookie balls in the freezer for about 20 minutes instead of overnight in the refrigerator, and that seemed to work. The trick is to pull them out of the freezer while you can still squoosh them down! Leave plenty of room for spreading, though; the ping-pong ball sized rounds spread quite a bit so I only fit 9 on a large cookie sheet.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This cookie is delicious & such a special treat. It is 100% something yummy & not at all a combination that is everyday. 
We all eat chocolate chip cookies & oatmeal cookies in every combination possible. 
Why not try something new? Honestly,  why not?
Most people don’t “Love” white chocolate but, 
This cookie is not your stereotypical flavor palate.

Get out of your head & into the kitchen. Grab your spatula & enjoy baking something new. 
Every baker reading the comments should try the recipe with the white chocolate & the freeze dried raspberries. 
If it isn’t your favorite, then give the cookies away.
Not a single person you’re gifting will complain!
2nd time around, tweak the recipe to your favorite chocolate preference.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Just made my first batch. I use a scale-much more accurate that way. Didnt want huge cookies so portioned out dough at 32 grams apiece for cookies which bake to about 3 inches across. I chilled, then baked for 16 minutes.  No trouble with recipe as written. Texture excellent and will adapt formula for Tollhouse cookies. But, Im finding that the freeze dried raspberry sprinkle at end-when cookies are out of the oven does not stick. So I’ll have to stack them carefully for freezing instead of tossing them in freezer bag.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Fabulous. I followed the technique of adding extra milk solids(powder) to up the flavor of my browned butter (Google it). I did not have the patience to wait for these to stand in the fridge for more than a half hour. I did not flatten the SCOOPS. Did not bother with the additional chocolate/raspberry sprinkle on top-just stirred it all in to the cookies. Baked at recommended time/temp. They came out PERFECT. Full of raspberry flavor and just the right amount of white chocolate. My second double batch is in the oven as I type this. Recipe is definitely a keeper.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
These are PERFECT.  I don’t have a sweet tooth, I was making them for someone else, and I think they are sublime.  I used white chocolate chips in the dough, smashed some with the flat edges of my knife for the sprinkling on top (resembling crumbled cotija cheese). I think the intent is that the chocolate on top melts and the additional raspberries will stick to that, but that does require some delicate pressing to get to work. 

With my smaller sized dough balls (1-1.5”), this yielded 36, 3” cookies.  It took 10 minutes in my oven to get the right result without over browning.

commentBody: These were so so good! I did a mix of white chocolate, milk chocolate, and dark chocolate chips for added complexity. commentBody: Good cookies. I used mini white chocolate chips because I had them. They worked great in the cookie, but it was hard to keep them on top of the hot cookies. Baked them 8 min. It made 47 two bit cookies.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I loved reading this thing for overcooked crunchy rice!  I am a 70+ half Chinese woman, and I discovered when I was about eight that when the pot of rice was left on the stove too long, the bottom got a bit crunchy.  I absolutely loved rice that underwent this accident, having no idea it was a "thing" in many cultures. I would often sneak into the kitchen and turn up the burner on the simmering rice while my mother's back was turned.  What a treat!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
1st cooking this,tastes great. Lessons learned: my leftover rice was still warm in rice cooker and I used a 12” cast iron skillet at low/medium heat. 

Rice didn’t stick together as well as cold rice. I would use medium heat to better scorch the exterior and keep the pancake  intact. Bottom releases well from cast iron but flipping in a hot cast iron skillet was not in the cards. I quartered it and flipped the pieces with a spatula and served.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Yes Made with pretty dried out brown jasmine rice. Used olive oil, added the tablespoon of water, and sprinkled with salt. It browned beautifully in far less than 15 minutes, more like 3-5 minutes. I flipped it over easily.

Unfortunately, I cooked it too long on the other side. It browned beautifully but no soft rice in the middle. It was more like a rice cracker so I sprinkled it with more salt and ate it by hand. It was delicious! I might use it as a canapé cracker for the future.

-Eric

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Italians have long cooked tomato vines in pasta sauces and other tomato foods.  They add an incredible depth of flavor. They are removed prior to serving, though.  Eileen is correct that there really is just a small amount of alkaloids (solanine and tomatine) in them, which in large quantities could cause gastrointestinal problems.  But that won't happen unless you eat them in such quantities.  Non-Italian chefs like Kenji Lopez-Alt and Jamie Oliver agree.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Absolutely incredible weeknight summer meal. I’m a bit surprised by how delicious this was with so few ingredients. I used homemade chicken stock from hainanese chicken rice, so it was filled with alliums, but I think any good store bought stock would be great. 

I did add two cloves of garlic and a bit of grated Parmesan to each bowl. Next time I’ll double the recipe to have some leftovers. I also feel like some blackened shrimp would go great with this since the flavors reminded me of shrimp and grits. I’m already planning on making this again next week. Thanks, Eric!

commentBody: I 2.5x’d the recipe as I had a 5 cup container of frozen homemade chicken stock to use. I used Arborio rice.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Tripled the recipe to make it as dinner. Made it vegetarian with paprika instead of bacon. Used better than bouillon veggie broth. Added two cans of chickpeas for protein, plus a side of crusty bread and a salad. The sesame oil was a nice add, but next time I think I’ll do a lemon olive oil drizzle instead, as a matter of preference. Next time, I’ll go lighter on the oregano or perhaps use Italian season instead. Family licked their bowls. Will definitely make this again. I had one pack of cherry tomatoes on the vine and one with out. It was fun to cook with the vines, and it definitely added a lil something. Easy and delish! You could definitely stir in some baby spinach at the end for a green.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: This is a fabulous recipe,  and  very easy, especially after a long workday. I got a bunch of Trader Joe’s  baby, cherry tomatoes on the vine, and didn’t even weigh them just used them all along with the stems and a sprig of thyme I happened to have in the tomato basket. Also doubled the ounces of bacon, use much more than a pinch of red pepper, and used more broth as someone suggested. I had some leftover mushroom  broth  in the fridge so used that to make up about a third of it, remainder was Swanson’s chicken. Everyone is right about the sesame oil being a masterful touch. The fresh basil from our garden is so fragrant right now it kicked it even higher over the top.  Thank you so much for this. It will go on very regular rotation in summer and fall.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Pro Tip: Separate the tomatoes from the vine before adding to the soup — it makes it much easier to remove them before serving. The tomatoes fall off the vine anyway while cooking, but they retain their calyx (green cap). It's extremely difficult to remove the cap from a boiling hot soggy tomato. I find it much easier to throw in the tomatoes (sans caps) and a couple of vines for flavor.

And I agree that adding tomato vines really does enhance the tomatoey flavor. Great tip.

Having it for lunch the next day and it tastes even better.  I added a fresh grated parm last night and am trying chili crunch today.  I see myself adding some sort of cooked egg with runny or jammy yolks.  A total keeper and one I see myself making again and again. So simple. So tasty. So foundational. Thank you!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Made this today with one small change (Jasmine rice had bugs in it, so Japanese short rice it was!). I used a cube of my homemade reduced stock and topped up the rest with better than bouillon chicken for the stock base. Of the two porky options, I chose bacon All in all, I think it was remarkably flavorful. I didn’t even feel the need to add salt, which is unusual for me. 

I had eaten some of the tomatoes ahead of time so I had a good idea of just how tomato we there were they were, and I do think that keeping the stems in enhance the flavor. I would definitely make this again, but I would try the jasmine rice.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I’m home alone sick on what is probably one of the last truly beautifully warm weekends here, so I decided to make this for myself. It was so easy and incredibly delicious. In my illness haze, I put my regular sized on-the-vine tomatoes right into the pot. If I wasn’t halfway dead, I would’ve halved or quartered them and put the vine in separately. Either way—yes, you can use larger vined tomatoes and still have an amazing soup. Don’t skip out on the red pepper or basil.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Not enough garlic in this earthy dish. Three-four cloves to two or two and a half cups of strained yogurt is standard in Greek households. It's meant to deliver a punch. Also, it needn't be fluffy, but it should be more delicious than this recipe produces. Most of all, no vinegar in a dish that deserves to be called tzatziki. And, yes, always good olive oil and fresh, chopped dill or mint. In Greece, this is a salad, often a meze. Never a dip, which is deeply anathema to Greek culinary ways.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Following others’ comments: I crushed up a sleeve of graham crackers (very coarsley to retain crunch), mixed with 1/2 stick melted butter, patted into a 9x13 pan. Stuck in oven for a few minutes as it was preheating. Topped with a box of brownie mix which I added a tsp of espresso powder to. Baked for 5-6 minutes on 350 and then added the marshmallows on top. Baked for another 15 minutes at 350, then raised to 400 for the last 5 minutes so the marshmallows could brown and puff up. Seems to have worked well! Brownie is fudgy and slightly gooey but not raw. Looks beautiful and tastes like s’mores!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Used 1 box of Ghirardelli brownie mix+2 oz. chopped 64% Guittard baking bar+1 tsp. espresso powder+10 oz. MINI marshmallows+crust recipe below, in a 9x13 with removable bottom. Pre-baked the crust @350 for 10 min. Reduced to 325 and used an offset spatula to spread the brownie mix (w/bloomed espresso powder+chopped chocolate). Baked for 25-30 min. Topped with mini marshmallows and broiled.

Crust:
340g Graham cracker crumbs
1 stick unsalted butter, melted
1/4 tsp. DC kosher salt
5 T. Brown sugar

commentBody: I made these yesterday for a cookout we had today, and I couldn’t believe the bake time I had to add. Even after an additional 25 minutes of baking, it was still jiggly, but the marshmallows were more than brown, and I was afraid they would be inedible. They went directly into the trash and I will ban it from my recipe box/collection.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Brought these to a BBQ and while it wasn't technically a contest, I won. Made a few changes that I'd stand by next time:

-1.5x the batter for a 9x13 metal pan
- Added a couple handfuls of chocolate chips to the batter for the texture
- Added 1 tsp instant espresso powder to the batter
- Baked w/o marshmallows for 10 min.
- Topped w/ whole bag of mini mallows and baked for another 12 min. until deep golden brown
- Sprinkled w/ flaky salt after removing from oven

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I baked these this weekend, and they were a huge hit! They taste just like a s'more. I unfortunately overbaked them a bit because I kept thinking they weren't baked. My advice to everyone who thought that their brownies weren't baking well: the marshmallows leak a sugar syrup as they get hotter, which leaves you to believe that the brownie layer isn't baked through (the syrup makes the brownie layer very gooey when you test them with a cake tester). I would suggest baking them for 5 minutes without the marshmallows, then bake for an additional 15 minutes once marshmallows are added. Then take them out and let them rest!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: After reading everyone’s tips, I made a standard graham cracker crust (155 g graham, ~ 7 T butter, 2 T sugar) baked that at 325 for 6 min, then poured in the Ghirardelli double chocolate brownie mix batter (the one from Costco) and baked for maybe 30-40 min until it was almost done. I removed the pan and cranked the temp to 425 then placed large marshmallows all over, giving them a little space in between as folks recommended, put back in the 425 oven for ~5-10 min until the tops were browned. It is delicious, but nearly impossible to slice. We’re just eating it by the spoonful from the pan. Thankfully I lined the pan with parchment and used an 8 inch pan with a removable bottom so nothing sticks. The marshmallow does stick to the parchment, but it hardly got on there. It’s so good and was relatively easy in spite of the 3 steps.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I used gluten free grahams, gluten free brownie mix, I had some bits of brickle (crushed Heath bars) I sprinkled on top, and totally covered it with mini marshmallows . Baked in a 13x9" pan for about 20-25 minutes till the marshmallows browned, refrigerated it when cool, cut the next day.  I had made 2 other, labor intensive kinds of cookies for the gathering and these easy gf ones were gone in a flash.  Marshmallows were crunchy around the edges and the brownie was fudgy--hurts your teeth thinking about all that sugar but people absolutely adored them.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: This recipe is great. If one wants to omit store bought marshmallows, as I do, use 3 egg whites beaten with 3/4 cup sugar (in the traditional fashion adding sugar gradually until very shiny and stiff- like in a baked alaska) and mound on top after baking and torch them just before serving! I imagine adding them on top during the bake likely also works… but haven’t tried that.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I’m kind of upset because these brownies for our family were just kind of “Meh”. I used.Guittard bittersweet baking chips.  I think the chocolate is almost too nice for this recipe. A Hershey tasting chocolate I think would be better.  I also used Trader Joe’s marshmallows and Trader Joe’s boxed graham crackers.  Marshmallows just don’t have that special original marshmallow taste that we all grew up with.  I love the idea that somebody posted about making a real graham cracker butter crust versus just putting the whole graham crackers and sprinkling sugar, etc..  The brownies themselves have a cake texture for me. So overall it’s kind of a miss and I’m disappointed.  I usually don’t get let down by five star recipes.  I put them in the fridge thinking maybe they’ll taste better tonight and will give them one more chance.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I listened to too many comments when making this recipe. Here’s what what went right:
- Graham cracker pie crust base, this worked well and was easy enough to do. But I do this based on whatever you have on hand. I happened to have crushed graham crackers so I opted for that over buying a pack of crackers.
- Use mini marshmallows or cut big ones in half.
- The brownie recipe is actually quite easy and simple to make, worth it imo (I believe store bought brownie mix will create much more batter than this recipe)
- Marshmallows at the end, I can see how putting them on at the start can be a problem, especially if your brownie bake time needs longer (see my bad note below)

The bad:
- Overbaked the brownie. Everyone is saying it never cooked through. I’m guessing many opted for a boxed brownie if that was the case. I baked mine for 20 minutes then did another 5 and it was overbaked but I still had to add the marshmallows. By the time those browned using the broiler real quick, the brownie definitely got drier.
- Overall the whole thing is a bit dry and tastes ok.

I’d give it 3/5. It’s not bad but if you don’t get the prefect bake on it, the whole thing is meh.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: When I was in the Navy, stationed in Japan, back in 1968, fried spaghetti was served by Japanese cooks in the Base snackbar. Everything, like hamburgers or hotdogs was cooked on the grill.  The fried spaghetti replaced French fries.  While your burger was cooking, a handful of pre-cooked and oiled pasta was tossed on the grill, given a few squirts of ketchup, and few flips with a spatula. All things considered, it probably began as a cross-cultural dish based on opportunity and supplies on hand.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I lived for years in Japan and fell in love with many of their versions of Western cuisine—washoku. It may sound horrible but with a few exceptions, most of the dishes are delicious including this one. Japanese Doria in all its various forms is also great. Castella cake, a sponge cake based on a Portuguese dessert is amazing. And to those sneering at this recipe, worried about sugar content, the Japanese eat much less sugar than we do. As in life, moderation is the key. The little sugar in this recipe is not going to kill you! And if you feel so strongly about it, why bother cooking the recipe or commenting on it?

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I lived in Tokyo for a little over a year, right out of college, over 35 years ago. It was an incredible experience living abroad and every day I would be reminded that I was not in a Western country. It made me appreciate living away from the US as well as returning. Japanese interpretations of other countries' food, such as this dish and Japanese "curry", AKA "yoshoku," as the article notes, are so unique and distinct that they shouldn't be compared to the "original," but considered in their own right. This dish took me back to my days in Tokyo, and I've made it twice in the past week. Easy, homey, simple & oishii ("delicious" in Japanese).

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I like it with cubed smoked kielbasa or batons of ham instead of bacon. Bacon in Japan is much leaner. You could also make this with minced pork or beef. Green bell pepper is good, but I often use poblano or Anaheim peppers as they're closer to Japanese green bell peppers. Peppers are the must-have ingredient for that quintessential Napolitan flavor. Also, plenty of the fake parmesan cheese that comes in the iconic green container, with a dash of Tabasco before you eat.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: This was easy & tasty. I used boneless beef ribs cut into 1/2" strips. It marinated covered for a couple hours on the countertop. Changes: add one more scallion + a star anise for additional flavor. I minced both the garlic & ginger. I thought about broiling the meat but went ahead & used the cast iron, as recommended. Fresh spinach leaves, flavored rice from another NYT recipe + steamed pea pods. I had no soju and used tequila! A fabulous dinner!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Recipe looks great! However, I highly recommend changing the cooking oil to a much higher smoke point oil such as avocado. Olive oil tends to smoke and “burn” when searing at high temps which can leave an unpleasant taste in the food. Avocado oil has a much higher smoke point and is very similar to olive oil in terms of favorable omega fatty acids. Also, I can’t imagine this wouldn’t be amazing on the grill! Excited to try!

commentBody:

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I loved this recipe! Thank you, Eric. No idea if anyone is wondering but this tofu’s really well. I made chicken for myself per the recipe and tofu for my husband. I used a block of medium tofu cut into small rectangles (marinated for a few hours) and after frying till crisp in a cast iron, I reduced the marinade down and poured it on the tofu after. For anyone complaining of lack of heat - this is a choose your own adventure scene and if you want it, add it! We added heat afterwards with chili paste to dip in when we crafted our bites. I also think sliced fresh jalapeños would have been great to add into the bites for extra freshness and a nice extra green feeling quality. I will do this next time.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Unfortunately, have to agree with the other reviews that found this a bit bland. The scallions were the best part. I seared the chicken in my cast iron to get it brown, removed it from the pan, cooked the sauce to reduce it, then added the chicken back and finished it in the oven. The sauce had more concentrated flavor but even still it felt lacking. Perhaps the apple juice made it too diluted. I also should have skipped the water - it seems a strange tip to tell people to use water if don’t have the alcohol. I think it would be better to just skip it all together.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Terrific recipe. The best grilled chicken breast I've made--flavorful and juicy. Note: the marinade is actually a brine. It's very, very salty (if any critique, possibly too salty--Morton kosher salt goes a long way). It's not a marinade in the sense that you could set aside a little before using to serve with the cooked chicken. When I tasted it I thought it would be a disaster. But it worked out. Still, I'd reduce salt to 1 1/2 tsp. I did add some oil to the "marinade"--no sticking on grill

commentBody: @Amy K No. Sake and mirin aren't interchangeable - sake is rice wine, while mirin is a fancy sugar syrup. author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I learned how to bake bread in Adult Ed class when I was still in my twenties. Much older now. We did not have stand mixers at our disposal. All you need is a Pyrex bowl and a wooden spoon. Mix up the ingredients and knead by hand until the dough is elastic and  tacky but not sticky. If too sticky gradually add a little flour. You’ll probably need about a good 15 minutes to knead by hand. Then, just follow the rest of the instructions. Great rolls, minimal cost.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: For those who are lamenting the whole stand mixer thing, think about it. Your grandmothers, and their grandmothers made bread by hand. I taught myself to bake bread when I was in my early 20's. Kneading dough for 10 minutes can be meditative. There is something about feeling the dough and the gluten development. When I entered pastry school at 53 I (almost) felt guilty using the 20 qt Hobart mixers. Anything that mixer can do, you can do! Get your hands in there and make your Grandma proud!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Love these  rolls! I use 158g AP flour & 21g cornstarch for the cake flour. I find that even without a mixer, this recipe comes together easily. I bloom the yeast in the water, then add the flour/salt mixture. I knead it for a minute or two in the bowl, then let rest for 20 minutes. After that, it only takes about 5 minutes of hand kneading to get a smooth, soft dough. Covering with well oiled plastic wrap on the last rise is important! Makes 16 nice sized dinner rolls. Baking time is spot on!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Made exactly to recipe, except sprinkled tsp more water while kneading as so dry. Everyone agreed very tasty, but dense.  I calculated the hydration % later and realized it was only 58.5%. Very dry.  Baguettes (mentioned in the intro to the recipe) are usually between 70-75% hydration and even as high as 80%.  I’ll make again using 73% water.  Will also try subbing a little whole wheat for the all-purpose flour.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Decided to go ahead and make this in spite of the mixed results reported. I am a regular bread baker. Followed the recipe carefully, though I did use closer to 3/4 tsp yeast. Formed the rolls and all looked good. Checked 1 hour later and the rolls had flattened and begun merging with each other. I reformed them so they looked like rolls, preheated the oven with a pan of boiling water on the lower rack, then popped them in. Baked for 20 minutes. They look like the photo. Taste and crumb are good.

commentBody:
Gluten-Free version for some of us:
½ teaspoon (2 grams) active dry yeast
1⅓ cups (300 grams) warm water (~110 degrees)
3¾ cups (513 grams) Bob's/King Arthur One-to-One Baking Flour
1½ teaspoons fine sea salt
2 tablespoons (30 grams) olive oil
1 tablespoon (21 grams) honey  (helps with browning and yeast activation)
1 large egg(for the dough)
1 large egg (for egg wash)
Nonstick cooking spray

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I'd call this great for hangover, influenza and covid!  It's so warming and delicious.  Like others I didn't want to boil out the goodness of the fermented kimchi and soybean paste.  I sautéed the garlic, ginger, gochugaru, fish sauce, and doenjang in 1/2 tsp of olive oil until fragrant - less than a minute.  I added the stock, 2 cans of beans, simmered, not boiled.  Next dropped the onions, kimchi - light simmer until everything warmed through. Cilantro brings it all together!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
So good, will definitely make again.

This recipe came at a great time - I have an abundance of homemade kimchi in my fridge. To keep it vegetarian friendly, I used  Better than Bullion No Chicken Base, and soy sauce   rice vinegar in place of the fish sauce. My kimchi was already quite spicy, so I used the gochujang very sparingly. I decided to add some frozen corn and peas for some color. In addition to cilantro, I added lime as a garnish. Served on rice to make it more of meal - and help balance out the ample spiciness.

commentBody: This might be my very favourite NYT recipe, and thanks to NYT my husband’s favourite food of all times. Made this dozens of times, at least once per week, and it’s gonna be a keeper for years to come. I make it vegetarian, so vegan fish sauce and homemade kimchi. Always make a huge pot, because this tastes even better for lunch the day after (and the day after that). We like to add some extra seasoned rice vinegar and sesame oil at the table. So insanely good. Thank you very much, Eric Kim! You’re considered a hero at our table. <3

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Saw this recipe and since I had a jar of kimchi I wanted to finish plus all the other ingredients I made it.  I added an extra can of beans and since it reminded me of a sauerkraut soup I’ve made I served it with a dollop of sour cream.  It was delicious and with all the other ingredients Mr. Kim used way more flavorful than the sauerkraut soup I had made.  The next day I added the remains of a can of chopped tomatoes plus some cheddar cheese on top and it became a mash up of Melissa Clark’s Cheesey chili crips bean bake.  It was equally delicious.  Thanks Eric and Melissa.  Would never have thought to do this combo of flavors on my own.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
This was amazing! I was a little nervous because some of the ingredients were new to me to cook with, but I followed the recipe exactly. Well, almost… I accidentally bought gochujang - which is a paste rather than flakes or powder - and didn’t realize til I was reading the tips AFTER I already made it. But
the whole family loved it, even my son who hates beans. Next time I’ll substitute chicken for the beans and keep everything else the same. We like things pretty spicy and it checks that box for sure. author: Eric Kim
commentBody: After making this several times, we like throwing 2-4 (4 if doubling the recipe) frozen boneless chicken thighs into the instant pot for 14 min. Use the leftover juices in the pot to the chicken broth called for. Shred/cut chicken to replace the beans (we don’t normally have canned beans on hand). The addition of enoki mushrooms with the onions is lovely. We serve with rice (in the soup bowl or on the side depending on individual preference). Everything is ready around the same time if I start the rice cooker when starting the instant pot. Fast, delicious, and easy prep work - this soup always makes my stomach feel better for whatever is ailing me!

commentBody: Jacques Pepin does the following:  Slash the thighs on both sides of the bone for faster cooking.  Put skin side down in a cold non-stick skillet.  Start on high heat and then lower heat & cover the pan.  Cook about 20-25 minutes and check for doneness. He makes a delicious vinegar pan sauce but you can make whatever sauce you want.  The chicken come out perfect.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: Make this recipe using Julia Moskin's technique for "flattened" chicken thighs: cook the thighs skin side down in a cast iron pan. Place another same-size pan (pref cast iron) over it, weighted with a full kettle. No spattering. The skin browns and crisps, the thighs are cooked  in  twenty minutes. Turn them over and follow the rest of the ecipe, cooking uncovered, removing from pan, and adding lime juice and maple syrup.  May as well use the lime zest too.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: This recipe rules. I served it with guacamole on little flour tortillas, with the lime sauce and a dash of hot sauce as salsa. Awesome. Will be a go-to in this house. Like other readers, it took me way longer than 20 to 25 minutes to finish the thighs. More like 45, though that may be my technique. And my chicken never stopped sizzling like in the video. (I have the same or similar pan that Kim uses, fwiw.) No big deal though. I babysat it and kept flipping, removed when I finally hit 180. It was quite worth it. The one thing I might change is the garlic, which tasted great. Recipe calls for just two cloves. Next time I’m using about 49.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: Once again a recipe that says 40 mins total took like 65 mins, but alas… I did mine in a cast iron skillet and even after almost 30 mins they were still poppin and spitting chicken grease all over the stove-top.   I must say, even though the whole place smelled like chicken fat (could be worse) and the cleanup was an ordeal, the crust and flavor was way more of a restaurant quality meal than I ever have had baking in oven, which yields a thinner less crispy skin.   I always use Better Than Bullion mixed with water for recipes calling for such a small amount of stock, and it came out excellent.    As delish as this was, I think it’s a one-off due to smoke/mess/super high fat quotient.   Very good recipe and excellent flavor of sauce too.  If I had a huge hood vent and a maid it would be on rotation lol.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was absolutely perfect as-is and will become an added favorite. My one and only recommendation is to double the ingredients for your sauce so you have plenty for leftover chicken. You will find yourself dredging your chicken in the extra sauce and licking your fingers if you’re not worried about table manners. It is THAT good. As to the “finish in the pan vs oven” debate, pan worked amazingly for me (I covered for the last five minutes after flipping—I’m a juicier chicken over crispier skin girl). It’s ultra important to know your stove and choose temp accordingly to garner golden skin, and prevent from burning your pan bits. For me, it was medium on a gas stove. This is a fairly messy dish to cook so it’s definitely a weekend meal for me when I have more time and energy for clean-up. Enjoy!

commentBody: My family really loved this. I cooked exactly as written and didn’t find that it took particularly long to get the chicken cooked through, as some other commenters did. author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: I start my chicken thighs in a COLD large pan on medium heat. After 20-25 minutes the skin is beautifully crispy and browned. I remove the chicken and add in a pound of fresh green beans, a few cloves of minced garlic, and the zest and juice of a lemon. I return the chicken on top of the green beans and place in a 400 degree oven for about 20-25 minutes. author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: Jacques Pepin talks about this on one of his Tiktoks.  He does three things differently - no pre heating the pan,  no oil in the pan - skin side down and the chicken skin will render it's fat, cover the pan with a lid and the steam produced will cook the other side and keep everything moist. I also try to salt the chicken skin and leave in the fridge uncovered for about 6 hours to dry out the skin a little more. Pepin calls this a method from Lyon his mother taught him. Highly recommended.

recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: This was amazing! My husband will not eat dark meat (thighs), so asked butcher in supermarket to remove bones from a package of breast’s (2 pieces) and thighs (4 pieces) but leave skin on. Since just the two of us, froze half of each pkg for future use. Followed Eric’s cooking directions to a tee, using an All Clad SS skillet and spatter guard and it turned out spectacular! Not much mess at all from oil/fat. I had the thighs and my husband had the breast. Crispy skin was perfect. Served with Jasmine rice with delicious sauce poured  on top. Husband loved it and I can’t  wait to make again with chicken parts that I froze for future use. Would not cha be a thing. TY Eric!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
This is an excellent recipe. Low effort with extremely impressive results. I cooked my chicken in a cast iron pan on an induction hob at 900 low and slow, just as Eric instructs us. I tuned into that low, deep sizzle that happens about halfway through cook time. I was able to easily check the chicken as the skin released easily from the pan. I used a thermometer and continued cooking till 30 minutes to achieve a 180 internal temp.  No burnt chicken here. The skin was golden, chicken perfectly cooked through and that sauce!! Wow. 
This was my first time cooking chicken thighs with this method and it will definitely be my go to from now on. 
Thanks Eric!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
I prepped the chicken thighs by placing on a rimmed baking sheet lined with parchment paper.  I liberally salted the thighs and placed them in the refrigerator uncovered for 3 hours.  

When ready to prepare the dish, I preheated my oven to 400 degrees and allowed the CB chicken to rest on the counter for 30 min.   

I drizzled a small amount of oil on top of the thighs and nestled 6 cloves of pealed garlic around the 6 thighs I was preparing.  Into the oven for 40 minutes.  I pulled the crispy chicken from the oven and removed the thighs to rest on a platter. 
I drained the juices from the pan, and used the dripping the base for the sauce following the recipe for the sauce as written.  I followed the recommendation of another post and used the zest of the lime as well.  I used fresh basil from the garden and served the meal with a salad and crusty toasted bread to enjoy the sauce.  
The meal was amazing. The sauce delicate and the maple syrup was delightful without overwhelming the lime.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
NYT food columnists compose great recipes. And great composers inspire others to produce variations on a theme. (Beethoven did that with Mozart.) I am no Beethoven but I have produced a variation on this recipe that is more than worth mentioning. I prepared the chicken quarter thigh and drumstick as described in the first steps but stopped when the garlic was added in preparation for the sauce. Instead I used a hollandaise sauce (100ml) to which I added 4tsp chives chopped to a length of about 1cm. I made sure the hollandaise had enough lemon juice to give a bright flavor. 

This is the time of year that white asperges is available. I used four stalks per person of skinned asperges cut into sections about 3cm long. I sautéed these in 1tbsp unsalted butter in a non-stick pan on medium heat until they were nicely browned. 

On the plate I put a pond—choose your own depth—of sauce, then the nicely-browned, crisp chicken on top of one side of the pond and the asperges sections on the other side. I then sprinkled an additional 2tsp of chopped chives over both the chicken and the asperges sections for both flavor and color. One could also use sautéed cherry tomatoes, again color and flavor. 

Wine? A Gran Reserva Chardonnay from Chile.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: This is a great recipe! I made it on the stove and found the oil still splatters. I wondered if this would work in the oven! And it does! You just make the sauce first and marinade the chicken with it in a bag. I did mine for 12 hours but you definitely can go 24. I turned my oven on air fry mode and set it at 400 (and I would imagine any air fryer work!) I cooked for 45 mins - I had a value pack of thighs (10) and guess what? Crispy perfection! Less mess too!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: Awesome recipe! I loved the technique lesson here on how to make crispy chicken skin & perfectly cooked chicken too (using moderate heat to brown instead of high heat)! I recommend to watch the video too. The chicken is ready to flip when the crackling stops — great tip! I used a weird halogen stove & it took 30 mins on the skin side. I used lemons instead of limes b/c it’s what I had. I also added chopped shallot when I added garlic — it helped lift some of the fond & added good flavor too. Eric, you are so awesome! Your recipe is relatively simple but it cooks up big flavor. Also, you are so great in your video: you are an excellent teacher & presenter, & also so fun & upbeat. Thank you!!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Okay folks, this was a miss for me. Followed the recipe and video scrupulously. Used a gas stove and a 12” AllClad stainless steel pan, heated, with 1 Tbs canola oil. Heat stayed at a mere twinkle under medium. After a whopping 55 minutes of cooking, the skins were still cemented to the bottom of the pan. Some spluttering still, not the sought after hushed silence but the thighs were at 180 by then and I was getting hangry. I scraped and hacked the chicken free, finished the sauce as directed. 
Sauce had glossy sheen and lovely drape. Garnished with fresh mint. Upon service, the chicken was quite overdone (no surprise) and the sauce just tasted odd. I think I’d prefer honey to the maple syrup. But am hanging up my apron on this one. Best of luck, friends.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Disaster! I've greatly enjoyed every one of Mr. Kim's recipes, except this one. Cooked this way the skin has a tendency to bond to the pan even in the best non-stick situations.  It splatters and makes a huge mess and then comes out skinless and undercooked.  Use a hot oven instead as other's have suggested (see Sam Sifton's Chicken Provencal technique for example).   "..shatteringly crisp skin"  ? The only thing shattering in this recipe will be your heart.
The sauce is still pretty good.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: Thank you, Eric Kim! You have the amazing gift of “cultural translation.” What I love about your recipes is that you uplift elements of tradition that don’t have well defined descriptors, terms, and narratives. I’ve eaten chicken prepared like this by my Puerto Rican relatives all my life. Usually seasoned with Goya Adobo, my Titi Helen’s chicken is melt-your-soul-delicious. I also love the SOUND of the crisps as the main reference for done-ness. Low temp and patience delivered! GREAT SAUCE!

commentBody: Yummy, Good Stuff! I made a few small mistakes, but it still turned out delicious. The sauce was tangy and velvety despite my little mess-ups. Don't skip the fresh herbs at the end.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
We have crispy chicken thighs at least once, sometimes twice a week. 
Because I loathe cleaning grease splatter off my cast iron stove grates, I use a  small pellet BBQ outside set to 475°F. I then season the thighs with salt and pepper and put them skin down on a dry, COLD cast iron skillet with no additional oil and put the pan on the hot grill for 25 minutes and close the lid. The fat under the skin renders and the thighs release with very little coaxing. Perfect every time. Can’t wait to try this version of the pan sauce. Sounds dreamy! 

Notes; if the ambient air temp gets below 35° in the winter, I add 5 minutes.
I will also trim the flesh to expose the bone a’la Jacques Pepín, for a more even cook.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: I made a vegan version of these w seitan & they're scrumptious! Eric Kim, you're right about that sauce. I want it w everything. To veganize: Steam seitan dough until cooked (I broke mine into med-sized pieces & steamed for about 25m). Put in container w lid, add cornstarch, & shake. Add oil & shake again. Cook in skillet, med-low, about 10m each side. Lightly-crisp outside, tender inside. Pick up recipe at Step 4, using vegan broth & butter. I ate w roast potatoes which adored the sauce, too!

commentBody: Now my favorite NYT chicken dish for simplicity and for it's wonderful sauce! After preparing it twice now, i realized it's important to: let the salted/peppered thighs sit for 15-30 min to render the moisture, assuring an amazingly crispy skin.  Also, for this wonderful sauce, be sure to reduce by half the stock, lime juice and maple syrup, before adding the butter.  I'm going to borrow an idea from Ali Slagle and her Skillet Honey Chicken, a very similar recipe, and add a chili with the garlic

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: There’s a lot of fretting over cook time, temp, and method in the comments, which had me a little worried, but fear not - Eric Kim’s recipe is pretty much spot on. Use normal sized thighs, not the monstrosities you often see in the supermarket (per-don’t, if you catch my drift). Cook them in a stainless steel pan over dead-medium heat, use a splatter screen if it spits too much for your liking, and add 2 or 3 minutes to each side if you feel it’s necessary (a meat thermometer is your friend). Serve with orzo, as others suggested, and you have absolute perfection. I’ll make this again and again.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Love the sauce. But to make this recipe even easier and less messy, lightly coat a pan with oil and roast the chicken in the oven, skin side up at 375, until skin is crispy, about 30 - 40 minutes depending on the size of the chicken pieces. You can even broil at the end to get the skin extra brown and crisp. remove the pan from the oven and put on the stove top. remove the check in and add the garlic to the pan. Saute the garlic and then proceed with the rest of the instructions for the sauce. 
Also, feel free to add other seasonings to your chicken besides just salt and pepper and make sure the chicken is very dry before adding the seasoning.

commentBody:
I probably should have read more comments first so o would have known to allow more time for cooking. I got beautiful brown skin cooking a bit longer than the recipe called for, but when we cut into the thighs they were still underdone. If I make it again I’ll try cutting the bones away as suggested by the Pepin fans, and allow it to cook more thoroughly. 

Not sure if we will bother again, though, the mess and fuss was more than I want for a weeknight meal, and the flavour was fine but not special occasion worthy. The NYT had featured this recipe on a list (something like quick and easy favourites?) but it didn’t hit that mark for me.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
Great recipe!  I incorporated several of the ideas previously expressed in the comments:
1.  De-bone the thighs.  They lay flat in the pan, evenly browning the skin and cook more uniformly.  They are also easier to wolf down.  
2.  Double the sauce.  No explanation required.
3.  Double the garlic and saute after the chicken has been removed to prevent too much browning.
I served this with basmati rice to soak up the fantastic pan sauce.

Thank you!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody:
This was great, but I had two issues. Cooking the chicken skin side down for 30 minutes produced crispy skin, but when I flipped them over, very little of the thighs made contact with the pan. I had to put them skin side down under the broiler for a few minutes.
The sauce was sublime but reducing 1/2 a cup of chicken stock in half left me with only 1/4 cup of the sauce. After dinner I went back to pan, added another cup of stock, more lime juice, maple syrup and garlic, and reduced that. Yum.

recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: I started the chicken in a cold pan with just a spritz of cooking spray. It took at least half an hour before the chicken was ready to release, and it was spattering just as vigorously when I pulled it off as when it started cooking. Flipped it over for five as the recipe said, then removed it. The skin was very crispy and golden, but the meat was overcooked and tough, especially under the skin. I won’t do this again — not worth it.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Crispy Chicken With Lime Butter Recipe
commentBody: I loved aspects of this recipe so much—the sauce is lip-smackingly delectable—but I had problems. Probably due to my lack of experience pan-frying, I struggled with cooking the chicken. Followed the instructions, kept heat medium-low, and 35-40 minutes later, skin deep golden and crisp, I attempted to release the thighs with a thin, metal spatula, and most of the skin stuck to the pan. Nooo! Maybe I used the wrong pan? (All-Clad stainless, but not nonstick)? I would make again, but in the oven, as many of you suggested, and maybe even use boneless and skinless chicken (heresy?) To me, what’s delicious is the sauce; couldn’t get enough!! Next time, double it.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: According to my partner who fancies himself a critic: If it’s Mac and cheese, it’s not cheesy enough. If it’s not Mac and cheese, it’s just weird. The best part was the variety of textures, he said.  I thought it was good but not enough to warrant five stars. Let’s leave the five stars to the truly spectacular. This was a decent weeknight dinner that gets the job done as long as you’re open to having a lengthy discussion about the use of linear versus logarithmic rating scales. Don’t leave out the lemon or nutmeg. Also recommend the arugula. It elevates this beyond just another tan meal.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Buckle up, pitchfork-wielding mob - I made a mockery of this recipe in the name of macros and I’d do it again. 

Subbed Brami pasta for regular, evaporated milk for heavy cream, skipped the last tbsp of butter, and added 1/2 lb ground turkey cooked with garlic and red pepper. Parm instead of pecorino because it was in the fridge. Kept all instructions and ratios the same (using the one-pot notes from others) and it was delicious. I’ll make sure I have arugula next time - because there will be a next time.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody: In forty years as chef, I would cook pasta in less water, 1-2" above pasta level, at high bowl, with no salt. At al dente set some water aside, then pour cold water into the pasta with its water. Works well. When ready to serve, drain the pasta, sprinkle with salt and EVOO, and mix into with the pan of hot prepared ingredients. Add grated cheese and some of the pasta water while mixing to the consistency you want. Season further to taste. It's all by feel and practice. Let the love in your attentive care take the dish to its ultimate level.

commentBody: After cautioning against over-salting the bean/pasta cooking water in the article accompanying this recipe, Mr. Kim fails to suggest an appropriate amount of each (i.e., of water and salt). America's Test Kitchen recommends one tablespoon of table salt per gallon of water and one gallon of water per pound of pasta. Serious Eats recommends an amount of salt equal to one percent of the weight of the water, which translates to about six and a half teaspoons of table salt per gallon of water.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody:
Another Eric Kim delicious dish, made as directed and among the best pasta dishes I’ve ever had. As others mentioned, be careful with the salt. I salt my water quite heavily b/c I like how the salinity infuses through the pasta and in this case the green beans too. Taste as you add some.of the water to the ragú and adjust with plain water. Also found I didn’t need quite as much water as the recipe called for… solution simply add incrementally bearing in mind you can add more. Orecchiette shape pasta really was perfect for this dish, albeit not essential per the subs. Eric mentioned. Lemon juice is important, and while I used bottled lemon juice, I’m very glad I didn’t leave the lemon out. Finally, while I was skeptical about boiling the green beans as long as I did, typically preferring a bit crisp, for this recipe the longer cook time turned out perfect for this dish, mine was probably 18 min. total, adding the 13 min. cook-time pasta after 5 min.
Fantastic dish! C

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody:
I'm a grad student on a budget, so ended up using canned, cut green beans, which didn't require boiling. Instead, I added them to the onion and sausage in the Dutch oven once the sausage was cooked, and boiled the pasta separately, and used chicken stock and the pasta water instead of the green bean water.
The result was quite excellent! I think the sign of a good recipe is its flexibility to accommodate budgetary substitutions, and the flavors were lovely with the fennel.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody: I used salted pasta water for many years since that’s what the Italians do. But once I got high blood pressure, I had to look for ways to decrease the amount of salt I use. Eliminating it from pasta water was an easy way to cut back. Think about it: our bodies need salt so of course anything with salt tastes better to us. But that’s also a conditioned response. The more salt we use, the more our taste buds become accustomed to it. Then when we eat something with little or no salt, it seems impossibly bland. Millions of people in the U.S. have high blood pressure, and not just old people. Rethinking some culinary traditions is one way to eat healthier, even if that goes against the accepted wisdom of the day. I still use salt, but in very small doses. The good news is that our taste buds can adapt to lower salt levels so it’s not a sacrifice.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody: My husband and I have found our perfect ratios for this dish. That’s not to say the original is “wrong.”  In fact, I can’t believe I decided to cook a dish with what I would ordinarily consider vegetables that are overcooked. This dish is a triumph. I will admit our ratios are different: we use 8oz of trofie pasta, 12oz green beans, 8oz sausage, somewhere between 2-3oz parm, the juice from half a lemon, and the zest from a whole one. On the whole, though, it’s the same. We just like more stuff in our pasta ,and the trofie is how we made it the first time around because that’s what we had. The loveliness of the dish is the technique and simple ingredients allow you to change ratios as you wish without really changing the character of the dish.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody: Salt increases the boiling temperature of water. Add it just before you add the pasta or other ingredients. Also, as I noted in my "Cooking to Beat the Clock" books, where meals take 15 minutes, not 35, cook the pasta an veggies together (first the pasta, then add the veggies. Following "package instructions" for pasta makes no sense here. Those instructions call for a lot more water than this recipe calls for.) While the pasta cooks, prepare the other ingredients, including grating the cheese.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody: Delicious! I love Eric Kim’s recipes, so I was excited to try this one. I didn’t cook it exactly as written; when I read 1lb of pasta I knew it would need more of the other ingredients. I used a little over a lb of ground Italian sausage to skip the de-casing step.  I also had some leftover mini peppers from another recipe, so I added those in when I added the sausage to the onions. Instead of green beans I used 1lb asparagus (an excellent suggestion from the author), simmered whole until just softened & set aside while the pasta cooked, then cut to size before stirring everything together. I used one & a half lemon, & god knows how much cheese I used. Finished with a handful of rough chopped parsley. I will make this recipe again!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody: Made this with homemade orecchiette (used 400g semola with 200g water for this dish) and it was wonderful! Only change I made: I put the green beans in the water pot when the sausage went into the Dutch oven... a little later than the instructions said. The beans were still boiling for 15 minutes and they tasted great.  I also grated the parm with a microplane ahead of time and it easily melted and incorporated with the pasta water and sausage fat to make a beautifully emulsified sauce.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Pasta With Green Bean Ragù Recipe
commentBody:
Add this one to your regular rotation! Such an easy, week-night crowd pleaser. I’ve made this one 4-5 times now always doubling the recipe and we never throw away the leftovers. Like the comments say, you’ve got to salt your pasta water, boil in huge pot with max amount of water and stir-stir-stir. Orecchiette pasta needs it because otherwise it sticks together and will not all be cooked completely or “al dente”. If you like the heat totally go for the hot Italian sausage plus the crushed red pepper to your palate’s pleasure. 
Everything about this recipe is fantastic, give it a try!

commentBody: Disappointing. Boiling beans + pasta together seemed like an ingenious shortcut but actually my orechiette snagged on the green beans and got sliced to ribbons. I threw in a bunch of fresh dill and oregano at the end, which improved it slightly.  The Parmesan/pasta water sauce was a nice idea, but I wouldn't make this again.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: We roast a 5lb chicken nearly every Sunday, eat the thighs and save the breasts for dishes such as this. But how anyone could get 6 servings out of this recipe as written is a mystery. For 3-4 servings I use 3 cups of Veloute, 1/2 lb mushrooms of any kind, 1 whole green bell pepper (sometimes a poblano for a little heat), a 4 oz. jar drained pimiento, dash of Worcestershire, 1/4 cup sherry and 1 cup frozen peas. Toast points become soggy; more fun to serve in Pepperidge Farm pastry shells.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I have a much easier way to make chicken a la king, which has been my favorite dinner since I was a young child. Stew the chicken. Make a roux in a saucepan (melted butter and flour). Add HOT broth to roux about 1 cup at a time, stirring with whisk until consistency is what you want. (It thickens a  little as it stands but can be thinned with more broth). Add sliced mushrooms and pimentos. Serve over chicken pieces and rice (or toast, or waffles, or chow mein noodles, or mashed potatoes,  etc.)

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Last week I made a classic recipe said to have originated with Paul Bocuse in Lyon. It’s called Chicken in Cream. Poach a chicken with aromatics. Remove the noble pieces to a warm oven, continue to simmer the off cuts until you have a fine stock. Strain the stock and add a scant pint of cream, temper 2 egg yolks and whisk into the stock. Pour over the reserved chicken parts and serve. This opened several possibilities for weeknight dining: a la king, pot pie, chicken and rice, chicken salad etc. etc.  the major benefit, Lucious moist chicken for many uses. Happy chicken!

commentBody: I roast a whole chicken often for my household of two. With leftovers I’ll either make chicken enchiladas or a variation of chicken a la king or creamed chicken served over biscuits or cornbread. It’s always a very pleasing dish, a nice reprieve from all the miso spiked or hot sauce concoctions that plague our culinary hedonism.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: @LS stick a knife in the thick breast bit (top) and assess the texture - if it's rubbery gelatinous, it's still raw. If it's cooked it will be firm yet soft tender. If the knife goes in comfortably, it's cooked. Juices will come out of the pierced meat are pale white, not pink or blood colour. Or go modern and use a digital thermometer (or a metal thermometer found in the cooking aisle), stick it in the thick piece and wait for it to reach 160-165F quickly (!). Remember the temperature will increase slightly as it rests, as the heat equalises - becomes constant. Resting meat (breast only needs 5 minutes) makes the texture better, moister because the meat locks in the juices, and residual heat in the meat will cook it further

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I grew up on this in the 1980s and 1990s. So, I've made chicken a la king for years. It's better with more veggies, and frozen or fresh veggies look and taste better in this dish than canned (it needs color, otherwise it looks kinda gross). Add corn, green beans, diced carrots, peas, and more. You can also substitute turkey. Rather than cream, I use 2% milk, and I don't use egg yolks or sherry. Still tasty to me with biscuits. Hearty winter dish that's adaptable.

commentBody: Made this successfully, it was quite good if a bit bland (hot sauce helps). author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Just want to second comments made by others regarding the thickening step. I had the sauce at a roiling, intense, boil for close to half an hour before the four cups of stuck reduced to a remotely thickened consistency. Reaching the thick texture of gravy you see in the picture would have taken even longer but by that stage my family wanted to eat and I was worried about burning the sauce on the bottom of the pot.

Not sure what the solution is here, given I am sympathetic to Kim's argument that the reduction is needed to deepen the flavour. I do think the recipe needs updating for how long the reduction will realistically take though. 15 minutes will barely cause you to lose 200ml of liquid much less get you a thick sauce that will cost the back of a spoon.

commentBody:
Would it be possible for the NYTimes cooking recipe developers be more mindful of the amount of sodium they are building into recipes?

While there is no obvious salt in this recipe, when I check the nutrition section there are 1400+ micrograms per serving.  When you are supposed to have less than 2000 per day, one serving of one dish is hard to have in your diet — and this looks like an interesting new to me recipe.

Also, the sodium is not coming from the vegetables or pork belly.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Thanks for the recipe! I grew up in Bergen County and would highly recommend Ewha Won in Closter, NJ. It's a short drive from Fort Lee and I think they make it best jjajangmyeon anywhere in the country.

I recently gave making my own jjajangmyeon a shot and have come to the conclusion that liquid smoke is an essential ingredient. It makes up for the lack of wok-hei with typical home burners.

commentBody:
Sadly my nearest Asian grocer did not have Korean noodles. I only thing I could find is Chinese wheat noodles branded as Yang Chuen. Since they are for soup and this is not a stir fry dish I hope they do the trick. The other option was buckwheat or Soba noodles but they seemed too different to the ones in this recipe.

Also for the Korean black bean paste, they only had roasted Korean black bean paste. Looks the same except for the roasted part. Hope it still works!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Here's my version inspired by Netflix food series and gives satisfyng result: I. Cook  jjajang in hot wok, remove jjajang, put it aside 2. Add pork, cook remove 3. Cook diced onion, chopped garlic, then add diced cabbage 4. Put pork & jjajang back to Wok, add a cup of boiling noodle water, season with salt, pepper and sugar. Bring to boil, adjust flame, until homogenized consistency 5. Serve over cooked, rinsed noodle along with julienne cucumber.(*Left over reheated jjajang sauce is as good.)

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Love the use of a cast iron pot to achieve the fond! I find it easier to fry the ChunJang in a separate pot to ensure I cook it thoroughly and remove the bitter taste, and then mix it in to the remaining pork and veg. I also typically clear out whatever odds and ends of vegetables are kicking around the crisper. This time I blitzed them into more of a fine dice/mince texture using the food processor to make a less chunky sauce - hoping this will help it stick to the noodles better!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Sizzled Bratwurst With Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
Just fry the bratwurst in a little bit of oil, no simmering in water. No German does this, except when you have Bockwurst or Wiener.

And to the mashed potatoes: Peel the potatoes before boiling them. And please never ever use an electric mixer for mixing them or will end up with what the Germans call “Tapetenkleister” (wallpaper glue). Use a potato masher or a ricer. Add a generous pinch of nutmeg and salt, no pepper.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Sizzled Bratwurst With Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
Having called the Rhineland Pfalz region home for 7 years, I can so vividly see the breakfast spread and would give my right arm for the brotchen and senf to serve bratwurst properly.
This recipe and preparation is quite authentic to the region. To get excellent genuine wurst in the US, seek out a company called Usinger's in Milwaukee WI. They ship, as does Neuske's, another fine WI purveyor of meats.

recipeTitle: Sizzled Bratwurst With Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
This is Sam Sifton’s brat recipe from about ten years ago:
Get some bratwurst at the market, some smallish potatoes, a couple of onions and the same count of bell peppers of various hue. Heat the oven to 375.
Cut the onions and potatoes into chunks, then seed the bell peppers and do the same. Toss in a bowl with a splash or two of neutral oil, then prick the brats all over with a sharp knife, add them to the bowl and toss again. Tip all that out onto a rimmed sheet pan, shower the ingredients with kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper, and slide it into the oven for an hour or so, shaking the pan around occasionally until everything's golden and crisp and soft at once.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Sizzled Bratwurst With Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
I grew up in Nordrhein-Westphalen, im Ruhrgebiet und Münsterland, 100% German. 

Mom and grandma never used water in the preparation of Bratwurst. “Brat” is derived from the verb braten (to roast), so preparation only involves roasting/frying. Get the process going with a little added fat. One the Bratwürste plump up, use a fork to poke holes into the casings so the sausage doesn’t burst and the rendered fat gets released. Once there’s a good amount of drippings in the pan, add 1-2 large inions, sliced into rings. Fry over medium heat until both onions and sausages are deeply colored. 

Traditionally, potatoes are peeled for mashed potatoes, but I skip that step. My family prefers waxy potatoes —the really waxy varieties aren’t generally available in the US, but Yukons are better than Russets by far. Boil chunks in salted water until done and drain. Add a pat of butter, a good splash of milk, more salt as needed, and some grated fresh nutmeg to taste. Use a potato masher to smash the potatoes- any remaining chunks are much preferred over “Tapetenkleister.”

The Sauerkraut needs to be drained and rinsed or it’s too salty and sour. It’s decent when braised in white wine with bayleaf, peppercorns, and juniper berries, and finished with a pat of butter. 

To serve, dish up mashed potato and Sauerkraut. Add the sausage on the side and pile a generous helping of the caramelized onions on top of sausage and mashed potatoes.

Guten Appetit!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Sizzled Bratwurst With Mashed Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
My Mama and Oma fried the Bratwurst (no liquid added). They used fork tines to poke the sausages several times on both sides to prevent the Wurst from bursting and to add fat and flavor to the pan. Alongside the Bratwurst they would caramelize 1-2 onions. Their sweetness against the savory backdrop of the Bratwurst juices is absolutely delicious, and we’d spoon them along with the rendered fat over the mashed potatoes. 

My family preferred “festkochende Kartoffeln” (firm potatoes) over Russets any day (Russets get too dry and mealy, Yukon Gold comes closest to preferred texture in the US but Geerman potatoes are even waxier than Yukons). Peel, half or quarter, and boil in salted water until done. Pour off excess water, mash potatoes with a masher, and add margarine (butter is next best), milk, and nutmeg. Gummy or “mealy” mashed potatoes are a big faux pas. 
My family preferred to rinse the Sauerkraut before braising it in a small amount of water (or veggie stock) alongside a bayleaf, a couple peppercorns and 2-3 juniper berries. 

Guten Appetit!

commentBody:
Things I recommend: For the potatoes, use a saucepan just big enough to hold the potatoes and water without boiling over, ~2 quarts. This makes the steaming stage more effective. The warm milk and butter keeps the potatoes from becoming gummy.
For the brats, sear first and then braise in the beer. I use a whole bottle of Shiner, braise the appropriate time, remove the brats, and cook the beer down until it thickens a bit. I season and pour it over brats as a gravy.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I have never had a stove that would go low enough to cook rice correctly on top. About 20 years ago I started to bake my rice at 250f and it turns out perfect every time. I turn on stove to 350. Start rice on stove top like normal. When rice/water is boiling I put in oven with tight lid and lower temp to 250. The rice can be left in the oven a long time without over cooking so you have time and room on the stove top to finish the mains.
White rice 20 to 30 minutes, brown rice 1 hour.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I have memories of my Japanese American mother cooking a pot of rice with a soup can on top to keep the lid tight. Get a rice cooker WAS liberating--not going back. If you only make rice once or twice a year, cook it in a pot. Any more than that, get a rice cooker. 

Rice cooker or pot method, do soak the rice. 30 minutes is a bit long, 8 hours excessive. I try for 10 or 15 minutes. Makes a big difference. Also fluffing and resting the rice is important too.  Uncle Ben's doesn't even come close.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
"Sushi rice" is not a specific variety of rice. Instead, it refers to cooked rice that has been seasoned for use in sushi, rather than being a distinct varietal. 

Calrose rice is classified as medium-grain rice, not short-grain. It is a domestic California rice and requires more water to cook compared to short-grain rice. While Calrose rice was once a staple in Japanese American households, where my mother used it, it has largely been replaced by higher-quality Japanese rice, which is now more readily available in the US.

The most commonly eaten rice in Japanese and Japanese American homes is koshihikari, which is used in a variety of dishes, including sushi. Typically, koshihikari is cooked using a rice-to-water ratio of 1:1.1, which means 110 milliliters of water for every 100 grams of rice.

Older rice requires more water to cook. I use koshihikari grown in Japan—it's important to note that all rice produced in Japan must display a Julian date code, as freshness is highly valued. 

For those in search of high-quality, freshly milled rice, Ubara rice is an excellent option. It is grown in Japan and shipped unmilled to a facility in Northern California, where it is milled and distributed.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: In Brazil, rice is a very important component of our diet. We cook it with onions and garlic, a bit of salt and the proportion of water is 2 cups of water to 1 cup of rice. We don’t “wash” the rice before cooking it. In my family, we don’t use onions, just garlic (fry it a little in the oil before adding the rice) and I like cooking it with vegetables broth instead of only water (1/2 cup of broth and 1/2 cup of water). You can add vegetables like broccoli or grated carrots to make it colourful

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: For Goodness' sake, my mom taught me/ her lowcountry people taught her how to cook rice. You use Long Grain. .don't have to "rinse" rice; they do that @ the factory [people these days are wimps]. Simply put 1 cup of water on to boil, with some salt in it. At the boil, toss in 1 c. rice, 1 stir, lid it, turn to the barest simmer, + set an alarm for 20 mins. When you hear the "Ding," turn off the heat + move the pot to a cool eye to sit. "Our" rice has never been clumpy!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: If you’ve had good Cuban food, you’ve had good white rice. Recipe is EZ & from the book-Memories of a Cuban Kitchen: 4 cups water, 2 cups long grain white rice, 2 TBSPs pure Spanish olive oil, 2 teaspoons salt. Combine all in large saucepan or pot. Bring to a boil on high heat, uncovered, until water has mostly been absorbed & small craters form on top of rice, 10-15 minutes. Stir rice w/ a fork, cover, reduce heat to low, & cook until the rice is fluffy.  Don’t lift lid during this step. Enjoy!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I use basmati rice, which is long grain. A colleague of Pakistani descent gave me the following instructions, which work out every time:
—Wash the rice in cold water
—Soak the rice (min 10 mins)
—Discard water then add 2x fresh water 
—Add flavourings (eg star anise, whole cinnamon, whole pepper corns, cardamon, salt, if I'm making dhal/curry)
—Bring to a boil then lower heat to medium and cook for 3 mins
—Turn off heat and cover pan with tea towel and lid
—Wait 15 mins, then fluff

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Basmati takes more water than Jasmine.  1.5 C Basmati to 2 C water and 2 C Jasmine to 2 C water in my RICE COOKER.  
Why not have a rice cooker?  National makes a basic, no bells or whistles, version that does all the work and you can ignore it while you use your time, focus, and stove top for the rest of your meal?  I am forever thankful that my Asian husband introduced me to the concept of the rice cooker years ago.  Our kids have turned on all their non-Asian friends as well.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Wow, this is way too complicated.  It's just rice.  Simplest method is to cook the rice like pasta -- bring large quantity of water to a boil.  Dump in rice and boil until rice is just tender, but a little al dente, about 8-10 min. for white rice, about 20-25 min. for brown.  Drain in a sieve.  In the same pot you used to boil the rice, bring about 1 inch of water to a boil.  Set sieve over the boiling water, cover and steam for 5-10 min. until fluffy.  No measuring, no soaking, no hassle.

commentBody: For decades I relied on brown rice and my rice cooker, unless I was making risotto, telling myself I couldn't make good rice. Most of the "work" happened when I was out hiking. What magic is this?!!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Here's a quick way to cook rice. Fill any large or small container with dry rice, depending on if you want a large or small amount of cooked rice. Use this same container to measure out approx. two liquid measurements (I use one of water and one of broth) I add about a 1/4 cup of olive oil and some salt to the rice. Place on the stove burner with lid on the pan, bring to a slow boil. Turn off the heat but leave the pan on the burner and the lid on 'til all water absorbed. Fluff rice with a fork.

commentBody: Many years ago I read in a small book on Chinese cooking that to cook rice, use twice as much water than rice, add rice to the boiling water and gently cook until little “craters” are seen on the rice. Still this is a much shorter method (probably around 40 min) than this recipe.

      1 cup of white rice
      2 cups of water

      Turn heat off and allow the rice to rest for 15 minutes
      Fluff with a fork and serve

For brown rice, double the cooking and resting times. Perfect every time!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I am looking forward to trying this when I have more time but for perfect, quick rice, I use a 2:1 water:rice ratio with a pinch of salt and a little butter. No rinsing or soaking the rice.  Bring the water/salt/butter to a simmer, add the rice, stir, put the lid on the pot and lower the heat.  Cook on low for 25 - 30 minutes with no peeking.  Turn off the heat, add a thick,folded tea towel on top of the pot and replace the lid.  After 15 minutes remove the lid & towel, fluff the rice and enjoy!

commentBody: I actually do almost exactly this (checked the recipe for ideas/ how I might improve)—I rinse Calrose rice in a sieve in running water and then let it soak while I walk the dog. commentBody: The method I use for rice varies from this one in one way. I sauté the rice in one Tablespoon of heated oil (coconut) in the heavy bottom pan, stir slightly until rice is coated and almost toasted, add liquid, bring to boil and stir and put on tight lid. Turn the heat down real low and cook about 16 minutes. Remove from heat, let sit for 4 minutes, Fluff rice gently with a fork, recover pan for two minutes or til ready to serve.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: I've been using a simplified version of this method to cook any variety of white rice for decades, and it's foolproof. For white rice, simmer over low heat for 15 minutes, rest 15. As for brown rice, simply double the times — simmer 30, rest 30. Fluff when finished. Rinsing is not necessary with most American brands of rice, nor is soaking before cooking — read the package to make that determination.

Before that, always burned the bottom of the pot despite following the recipe on the package by the letter.   Carolina Long grain rice, soak stir and rinse a few times. . 1-1/3 C of water to 1c rice. Once the water boils, cook on low 15 minutes. Tuen off the heat for 10 minutes without lifting the cover and fluff. Perfect every time. But lost on how 1 cup of uncooked rice plus 1-1/3 cup of water equals to about 3C of cooked rice.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Fran is correct; you do not need to spend 1 hour, 20 minutes making rice.  I learned something close to her method from a friend from Afghanistan, and it works beautifully. Key is to rinse the rice until water runs almost clear, and it will turn out fluffy without stickiness.  We can’t waste water in Colorado, so I fill large saucepan with cold water and 3 cups rice, then almost empty water and refill.   It takes about 6 passes to reach that perfect stage for cooking. Then cook in 2 stages.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
As someone who likes to experiment, cooking rice is an a complicated relationship. When it’s great, it feels so easy and you think why don’t I do this every time. When it fails you say never again and buy a rice cooker. Then the cheap yet brilliant piece of engineering starts to fall apart, nonstick coating shows its age and you toss it to the curb. You go back to the le crueset and stovetop, thinking how hard can it be? A couple bad batches later you’re cursing why rice isn’t as easy as cooking pasta. 

Anyway I’m here to say, I’ll try all the rice recipes. Thank you for this low and slow variation. I look forward to trying it.

Perfect.

commentBody: I had some shocking things to say after seeing this but I will refrain. I would seriously recommend to anyone who loves rice to get a Japanese or Korean brand rice cooker.
