Best part is, you don't have to watch what's going on and the result is nearly always better than stove-top rice.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Delicious, will make again. These are the modifications I made after stalking the comments section: added 2 cloves minced garlic with the onions, doubled the oregano, added scant tsp of dried red pepper flakes (perfect amount of heat, but this is truly super dependent on personal preferences), added some Tuscan kale I needed to use up after bringing the clam juice to a simmer. Totally forgot to add the basil at the end even though I had it - will try next time but the sauce was so good, I didn’t miss it. I served it with bread but would also be great over pasta or rice. Happy cooking!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Sharing a much faster version using the same ingredients, achieving a rich fish taste, but omitting the pesky clam juice that folks neither had nor wanted. I also used skinless salmon because, for me, the oily bitter taste of fried skin overwhelms the salmon's delicate flavor. I cooked tagliatelle first, used the pasta water to dilute the tomato paste, and in the same pot, assembled the sauce in which I cooked the salmon for 7-8 minutes. The sauce reduced while the salmon became buttery. Delish

commentBody: Made this last night and partner and I thought it was delicious. As our evening progressed, we both felt the dish was quite rich, maybe too rich! Perhaps substitute something else for the cream, or at least part of it. Made ours in a non-stick pan and it cooked up nicely, but after adding the sauce at the end, it wasn't crispy any more. Is it worth the frying - the splatters, smoke? I don't think it would be any different if you just prepared the salmon in another way and added the sauce.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
I told my husband this was a recipe called "marry me salmon" and after one bite he asked me to marry him again, twice! 😆 I don't normally invest the time to create such an amazing sauce (no time on week nights!), but we decided on a cozy Saturday night in and have nicknamed this "Saturday night salmon." 

I forgot to buy sun-dried tomatoes, so added extra tomato paste and olive oil and it was delicious. The sun-dried tomatoes won't be forgotten next time as I'm curious to find out if they add depth of flavor. Hope this helps!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: This was delish. But as the gentleman mentioned re a cast iron pan, I would add that even with the nonstick you need to cool the pan down a bit before throwing the onions in or they're likely to cook too quickly and/or burn. Also, I used half and half (that's all I had). Served in large bowls,  placed over large helping of sauce, with cooked veggies on one side of the salmon and cooked rice on the other. Skipped the basil, glad I did because the piquancy of the sauce would have neutralized it.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Hi! These were good and I recommend but I would not boil them again. I made four different NYT dumpling recipes, this was the only one that asked you to boil then fry. I was not able to get them dry enough (sorry aunt Georgia!) to prevent splattering and then sticking. In future, I will cook like the other, straight into the oil in pan and then maybe add a few drops of water to steam. Wondering if anyone had similar or has advice? Otherwise, delicious and a nice lighter addition. Thanks!

author: Eric Kim
commentBody:
Has anyone tried something similar with rice wrappers? I can’t digest gluten but this filling sounds delicious. I recently found small square and circular rice wrappers similar to wonton wrappers in size. I have seen some videos online in celiacs forums replacing the two for certain recipes, but it fails for a lot of dumpling fillings (especially more moist ones) and/or changes the texture and flavor too much. But this square shape and filling with the noodles makes me think it’s possible. 

Tips or thoughts before I go out and buy all the ingredients? 

I guess I can always just eat the yummy looking filling over rice if it fails miserably (and what is my cooking journey but a series of failed attempts to recreate my favorite dishes until I come to my own version of them?).

commentBody:
Tried these last night, some thoughts:
After boiling and microwaving, the dumplings started getting really sticky with each other before I could get them into they frying pan.  Next time will try to make sure they don't touch each other before frying.
I also had two pots for boiling dumplings and then two pans for frying. So I will enlist help for one of those stages and go from the pot to the fryer with as little time in between. (60 dumplings at five at a time takes a long time!)

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Awesome recipe but made a few mistakes worth sharing: used homemade, very flavorful and ‘very ripe’ kimchi as suggested but the flavor didn’t come through as expected. I did drain the kimchi, possibly too much as I was worried the filling might become too heavy for the delicate wonton wrappers. The noodles soak up a ton which I didn’t know as this was my first experience with sweet potato glass noodles. Also thinking the short boil may flush out some flavors so need to take that into consideration with spice amounts next time. Can’t wait for my second try around as I love the idea of kimchi filled dumplings. Go Eric, love the video!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Gukbap (Beef and Bean Sprout Soup With Rice) Recipe
commentBody: Kim's eloquent essay inspired us to make this "nuzzle you from the inside" soup with what we had on hand--replaced brisket with bottom round, scallions with thin-sliced onions, radish with cauliflower, bean sprouts with small-diced potato. And unlike so many other NYT recipes where folks feel divisive over substitutions, the warmth in our bellies (in part due to doubling the gochugaru) made us feel pretty confident we achieved gukbap perfection. It's a healing meal, after all.

commentBody: delicious. made almost as written. flatiron (it's a tender and affordable cut), a 3 inch square of kombu instead of sun dried tomatoes (removed later), halved garlic cloves and the scallions (left in), two stubby purple daikon, threw in a couple of small dried shiitakes (for more umami), and a bit more gochugaru + fish sauce (because, to taste!) - the only weird thing was substituting thinly chopped radicchio for the bean sprouts, because I had it. flavorful broth, tender vegetables and beef.

commentBody: This was good but not the flavorful soup I expected after reading the article. I also added a lot of extra fish sauce but something was missing. author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Gukbap (Beef and Bean Sprout Soup With Rice) Recipe
commentBody: This soup recipe reads delicious, warming and comforting.  But, a pound of flatiron steak was north of $17 last week at my nearest purveyor of edible meat (not a national big box grocery).  And brisket doesn’t come ‘by the pound’ in most of this country.  To justify the cost, this meat would have to be grilled to juicy medium-rare or long-braised for meals that would feed my household for a couple of days.  Sorry to pass on what reads delicious, but doesn’t pass the economic test

Would it not be easier to look for vegetarian recipes instead of trying to substitute ingredients that cannot be substituted?

recipeTitle: Olive-Oil Mashed Potatoes With Bay Leaves Recipe
commentBody:
In terms of texture development, this is pretty close to a Cook's Illustrated recipe from around 2004:  Start with at least somewhat waxy potatoes, mash gently (no electricity!), add fat (lots!) before liquid, don't over-work. That's the path for a velvety texture. If you prefer a texture, that's a whole different recipe (beginning with a starchy potato like a Russet).

I look forward to trying the additions...  Bay leaves, olive oil, and Panano, yummm!

When warming the milk and bay leaf, I add 1 or 2 crushed garlic cloves and a few peppercorns.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Olive-Oil Mashed Potatoes With Bay Leaves Recipe
commentBody:
At the time of this writing, I note that the vast majority of the 66 comments are from people who have not made this recipe. I made this recipe with great anticipation, following the fanfare the Times gave it, but my wife and I were both disappointed. It seemed to both of us that the ratio of fat to starch was way off. Four pounds of Yukon gold potatoes turned out to be nine potatoes. Between the olive oil, the butter, the fat in the cheese, and the butter fat in the whole milk, this recipe adds approximately a cup and a half of pure fat to nine modestly-sized potatoes. The result seemed to be a potato-flavored thick  pudding. I love fat as much as anyone, but the sheer amount yielded an eating experience a bit like bisque - it tasted like if you eat too much, you’ll get sick. :) Plus, the distinctive additional flavor of so much extra-virgin olive oil made these potatoes taste much less potato-y. For me, the highlight of the dish was the technique of adding the bay leaves to the simmering milk, which produced a wonderful flavor, and I’ll definitely be using that in other mashed potato dishes.
I know that tastes vary, so I will be interested to see if others who make this dish have a different experience. In my opinion, a rare miss from the Times.

I know that tastes vary, so I will be interested to see if others who make this dish have a different experience. In my opinion, a rare miss from the Times.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Olive-Oil Mashed Potatoes With Bay Leaves Recipe
commentBody: While this is really good as a standalone dish or with vegetables and fish, it might be too heavy with a hearty meat dish. My tip: Use some of the cooking water and less fat (butter)! True, the flavour comes with the fat, but substituting some of the milk and butter with cooking liquid will not only give you a lighter, but also a fluffier mash, even with cold butter. And since the liquid is salty, you probably won't need extra salt and the salt distributes evenly when mashing.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Olive-Oil Mashed Potatoes With Bay Leaves Recipe
commentBody:
After peeling and cutting up potatoes, I cover and soak them in water to remove the starch, which is what gives them a gummy texture if you're not careful. I change the water 2X. If I have time, I STEAM the potatoes in a double boiler, which also helps eliminate the starch.
I live in Perú, where ALL the potatoes in the world originated and there are currently about 4,000 varieties. No Yukon Golds, though. I'll try to pick one that isn't so starchy. This recipe sounds delicious!!!

commentBody:
One big mistake is filling the saucepan with water. Wrong. Use just enough water to all boil away and the potatoes are cooked. No salt is needed, but two or three cloves of chopped garlic is absolute. Timing is everything in cooking. So boil it dry. The recipe above has way too much water. Why tip all that potato flavoured water down the drain. 
Finally mash, whip add butter cream, parmesan, whatever you want.
This is the best mash you will ever eat.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Olive-Oil Mashed Potatoes With Bay Leaves Recipe
commentBody: So delicious! I used a fork to mash and it was fluffy and a bit chunky (perfect). I made ahead and reheated with some of the leftover bay leaf milk. It was still delicious, but lost some of the fluffy texture I got right after mashing with the fork. I reheated in mwave as opposed to the recommended stovetop. Not sure that made any difference in the texture though. It's kind of messy to be doing last minute with guests, so I would sacrifice a bit of texture next time and still do ahead.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Aglio e Olio Baked Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Roll your spuds! Trust me - they fluff up in the jackets! Once baked, don't cut them right away, but roll them between your hands when they are still piping hot (using mitts if you don't work in a foundry). This lightly mashes them and steams them a bit so they come out pillowy soft. I cut them in half at the equator and scoop out the jacket, dressing the mound of perfect flesh and each skin to my heart's desire. Butter, sour cream, chives or green onions, salt and pepper is all a spud needs :)

commentBody: If you don't have over an hour for a potato, my favorite baked potatoes are: 1 Large Russet, cover in olive oil and sprinkle with kosher salt, wrap in a soggy paper towel (Not just damp, completely wet and ensure the whole potato is covered). author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Aglio e Olio Baked Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Great recipe and easy to play around with variety of ingredients to your taste and what products you have or tend to use. I had Kewpie mayo open so I used that instead. Yum. Ran out of parm so I used a lot of mozzarella. When I roast garlic bulbs, I usually do 7 or 8 entire bulbs so I have plenty of roasted folic to use… and I used plenty of roasted garlic in this recipe instead of minced fresh garlic. I scooped out the potato “meat” and mixed it into the other ingredients. Yum yum yum. Put it back in the potato, then the oven to stay warm until ready to serve. No leftovers…I even ate the potato skin. Thanks!

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Aglio e Olio Baked Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
Solid recipe that’s very flexible to riff on, too. On a second round of it I cut the mayonnaise with an equal ratio of nonfat Greek yogurt to lower fat/calories while upping protein. No one knew the better. I have also amped up the aglio e olio flavors using the TJ’s seasoning of the same name instead of just salt. It layers into everything else well. 

Definitely use an olive oil you enjoy eating and let it flow freely and liberally.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Aglio e Olio Baked Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: We must’ve done something wrong because it just tasted like a potato with a lot of mayo to my partner and me. We will not be making this recipe again. First, the red potatoes are on the smaller side, so you have to eat more than one. Second, this was not a quick dinner. Even though we microwaved the (no-larger-than-my-fist) potatoes for 5 minutes, they still had to bake for about 35 minutes, and then we had to wait 10-15 minutes for them to steam. Third, we grated the garlic and Parmesan with a microplane and added plenty of chopped scallions and Maldon salt, but every bite just tasted overwhelmingly of mayo. Sad it didn’t turn out as expected.

recipeTitle: Hot Honey Baked Sweet Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: I didn’t have well behaved sweet potatoes. I had two that were long and carrot shaped, and a third that looked like an eggplant. Served 1/2 c mashed sweet potatoes with 1/4 c cheese sauce, a tablespoon of hot honey drizzled over the top with a sprinkle of salted Pepitas. Oh my goodness. Vegetarian main dish or dessert - you pick. Lovely recipe and a true keeper.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Hot Honey Baked Sweet Potatoes Recipe
commentBody:
I was feeling scientific, so I made a small batch of the goat cheese cream as well as another of blue cheese cream, each paired with the toasted pecans and hot honey, plus finely chopped sage or rosemary from our garden.  Each of the four combinations was delicious, but the winner for me was the goat cheese cream with sage.  It was sweeter and milder and the flavors melded together really nicely.  The strong blue cheese I used made it a much more savory dish.  My husband, who is iffy with blue cheese, said he liked both equally.  

I got scared when making the hot honey and reduced the cayenne to 1/4 tsp and the hot sauce to 1 tsp.  The honey by itself tasted plenty spicy, but once on the potato the spice was lost.  I’ll be brave and use the full amount of cayenne and hot sauce next time.  I used Frank’s and the honey was delicious by itself.  

This is a really filling dish so unless you’re really hungry or are having a dinner party, you may need less for the rest of your dinner than you think.  We both described it as “luxurious”.

author: Eric Kim
recipeTitle: Hot Honey Baked Sweet Potatoes Recipe
commentBody: Sensational!  So good.  This has supplanted my long time favorite “Sweet Potatoes with Tahini Butter” as my new favorite way to eat sweet potatoes.  I modified the recipe to use what I had on hand.  This includes a lot of vegan products as I am vegetarian and lactase deficient, so I have trouble digesting some cow’s milk products.  Used vegan heavy cream (Country Crock) and vegan butter (Earth Balance).  I had no hot sauce, so I used sriracha instead in the hot honey.  Had no fresh rosemary, but I did have fresh sage, so I used that instead.  My oven is hot, so my small sweet potatoes were done in less than 50 minutes.  Otherwise, followed the recipe.  Will happily make this again.   It was easy, fast, and extremely delicious.

Rich & very tasty, saved some calories, less cleanup & time.

author: Eric Kim
commentBody: Meh. It’s OK. I know all these New Yorkers are losing their heads of this. Must be the scoop of warm nostalgia poured on top. When I made it this evening, the sponge cake cooled and shrank 1/2 inch from the edge. Boo! Next time I’d either use an 8” springform or 1.5 x the sponge cake recipe. Probably the latter. Adding some lemon zest in the cheesecake (as well as the sponge cake) gave it some depth. I highly recommend Gina Marie brand cream cheese, if you can find it (or their farmer cheese, which is similar but with cultures for some tang). There no fillers, goo, gums or carrageenan, just milk, cream, salt (and kefir).  Made a meyer lemon curd for serving.
